# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why the M/M/1 Queue Has $L_s = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho}$

This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU ESE for Module 4. We will show every step explicitly.

### Step 1: State the Steady-State Difference Equations

For a single-server queue (M/M/1), arrivals follow a Poisson process with rate $\lambda$, and service times are exponential with rate $\mu$. In steady state ($t \to \infty$), the probability $P_n$ of $n$ customers in the system satisfies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda P_{n-1} + \mu P_{n+1} &= (\lambda + \mu) P_n, \quad n \geq 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** When the system is in state $n$, the *rate of flow into* state $n$ (one arrival from state $n-1$ at rate $\lambda$, or one service completion from state $n+1$ at rate $\mu$) must equal the *rate of flow out of* state $n$ (one arrival at rate $\lambda$ or one service completion at rate $\mu$).

### Step 2: Define the Ratio $\rho$ and Substitute

Let $P_n = \rho^n P_0$, where $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$. Substituting this ansatz into the difference equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda \rho^{n-1} P_0 + \mu \rho^{n+1} P_0 &= (\lambda + \mu) \rho^n P_0
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing throughout by $\rho^{n-1} P_0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda + \mu \rho^2 &= (\lambda + \mu) \rho
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Apply the Normalization Condition

The probabilities must sum to unity: $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P_n = 1$, which gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \rho^n P_0 &= P_0 \cdot \dfrac{1}{1-\rho} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** This is a geometric series valid only when $\rho < 1$. Solving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= 1 - \rho
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Average Number in System $L_s$

The expected number in the system is the expectation of a geometric distribution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_s &= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n P_n = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \rho^n (1 - \rho)
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the standard geometric sum identity $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n x^n = \frac{x}{(1-x)^2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_s &= (1-\rho) \cdot \dfrac{\rho}{(1-\rho)^2} = \dfrac{\rho}{1-\rho}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Derivation Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,L_s = \dfrac{\rho}{1 - \rho} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu - \lambda}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** As $\rho \to 1$, the queue length grows unboundedly. This is the famous "traffic intensity" insight — a system operating at 95% utilization has, on average, 19 customers waiting!

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mixed Strategy Solution of a 2x2 Zero-Sum Game

### Step 1: Set Up the Payoff Matrix

Consider Player A's payoff matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Equate Expected Payoffs to Remove the Dominated Strategy

Let Player A play strategy 1 with probability $p$ and strategy 2 with probability $(1-p)$. Player B chooses columns 1 and 2. For A to be indifferent (which defines the optimal mixed strategy):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(\text{column 1}) &= E(\text{column 2}) \\
p \cdot a + (1-p) \cdot c &= p \cdot b + (1-p) \cdot d
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Solve for $p$

Expanding both sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
pa + c - pc &= pb + d - pd \\
pa - pc - pb + pd &= d - c \\
p(a - b - c + d) &= d - c
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,p = \dfrac{d - c}{(a + d) - (b + c)}\,}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Game Value $v$

Substituting $p$ back into either expected payoff expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= p a + (1-p) c \\
  &= \dfrac{d-c}{a+d-b-c} \cdot a + \left(1 - \dfrac{d-c}{a+d-b-c}\right) c \\
  &= \dfrac{a(d-c) + c(a+d-b-c)}{a+d-b-c} \\
  &= \dfrac{ad - ac + ac + cd - bc - c^2}{a+d-b-c} \\
  &= \dfrac{ad - bc}{a+d-b-c}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,v = \dfrac{ad - bc}{(a+d) - (b+c)}\,}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Module 4 Tool

| Problem Symptom | Recommended OR Tool | Justification |
|------------------|----------------------|----------------|
| "Customers are complaining about long wait times" | **Queuing Theory** | Models the arrival-service imbalance directly. |
| "Our old machines are breaking down too often" | **Replacement Model** | Computes optimal replacement age minimizing total cost. |
| "We have 5 jobs and 3 machines — what's the best order?" | **Sequencing (Johnson's Rule)** | Minimizes total makespan / idle time. |
| "Our competitor just slashed prices — how do we respond?" | **Game Theory** | Models the strategic, interdependent nature of the rivalry. |
| "We have a single bottleneck server crashing repeatedly" | **M/M/1 with replacement** | Hybrid: queue length + failure rate combined. |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions in KTU 2024 scheme are direct, definitional, and require concise (3–5 line) answers. Each carries **3 marks**.

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Explain briefly the importance of Operations Research in modern decision-making.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Operations Research (OR) is indispensable in modern decision-making because it (i) provides a scientific, mathematical alternative to intuition-based decisions, (ii) enables optimal allocation of scarce resources such as capital, labor, and time, (iii) handles complexity involving thousands of variables that exceed human cognitive capacity, and (iv) quantifies trade-offs between competing objectives (e.g., cost vs. service level). By using models from queuing theory, replacement analysis, sequencing, and game theory (Module 4 tools), OR delivers measurable improvements — typically 5% to 25% efficiency gains — in industries ranging from banking to manufacturing. *(3 marks: 1 for scientific nature, 1 for resource allocation, 1 for quantifying trade-offs.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**List any four real-world applications of Queuing Theory in engineering systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Four real-world applications of Queuing Theory are:
1. **Bank teller window design** — determining the number of counters to minimize customer wait time.
2. **Hospital OPD scheduling** — allocating doctors to handle patient arrival rates.
3. **Call center staffing** — matching the number of agents to call arrival rates.
4. **Toll plaza lane management** — deciding the number of booths to prevent traffic congestion.

*(3 marks: 0.5 per application × 4 + 0.5 each explanation, total 3 marks. Adapt scoring: 0.75 mark per application.)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Each sub-part is **7 marks**. Below are two alternative questions: **Question A** and **Question B**, each with full sub-parts and step-by-step model solutions.

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **phases of an Operations Research study**, with emphasis on problem formulation and model construction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A small bank has a single teller counter. Customers arrive at the rate of **10 per hour** following a Poisson process, and the teller serves at the rate of **15 per hour** (exponential service times). Use **M/M/1 queuing model** to compute:
   - (i) Utilization factor $\rho$
   - (ii) Probability that the system is empty
   - (iii) Average number of customers in the system $L_s$ and in the queue $L_q$
   - (iv) Average waiting time in the queue $W_q$ **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Phases of an OR Study (7 marks):**

The six standard phases of an Operations Research study are:

1. **Problem Formulation (1.5 marks):** This is the most critical phase. The analyst identifies the decision variables (e.g., number of servers, replacement age, sequence of jobs), the objective function (e.g., minimize cost, minimize wait time), and the constraints (e.g., budget limits, machine capacities). A poorly formulated problem yields a useless model.

2. **Data Collection (1 mark):** Real-world data is gathered — arrival rates, service times, cost parameters, failure rates. Accuracy of input data directly determines the quality of the OR solution.

3. **Model Construction (1.5 marks):** The mathematical model is built using OR tools (e.g., linear programming, queuing equations, payoff matrix). The model abstracts the real system into a solvable form.

4. **Solution (1 mark):** The model is solved using analytical methods (formulas, Simplex, Hungarian algorithm) or numerical/simulation methods.

5. **Validation (1 mark):** The model's output is tested against historical data or pilot studies to confirm accuracy.

6. **Implementation (1 mark):** The validated solution is deployed in the real system, with continuous monitoring for refinement.

---

**Part (b) — M/M/1 Queue Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\lambda = 10$/hour, $\mu = 15$/hour.

**(i) Utilization factor (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu} = \dfrac{10}{15} = \dfrac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Probability system is empty (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= 1 - \rho = 1 - \dfrac{2}{3} = \dfrac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Average number of customers (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_s &= \dfrac{\rho}{1 - \rho} = \dfrac{2/3}{1/3} = 2 \text{ customers} \\[6pt]
L_q &= \dfrac{\rho^2}{1 - \rho} = \dfrac{(2/3)^2}{1/3} = \dfrac{4/9}{1/3} = \dfrac{4}{3} \approx 1.333 \text{ customers}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Average waiting time in queue (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_q &= \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)} = \dfrac{10}{15 \times 5} = \dfrac{10}{75} = \dfrac{2}{15} \text{ hours} \\[6pt]
W_q &= \dfrac{2}{15} \times 60 = 8 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Error 1:** Students forget to convert hours to minutes in $W_q$ (loses 0.5 mark).
> - **Common Error 2:** They compute $L_s$ but forget $L_q$ (loses 1.5 marks).
> - **Common Error 3:** They write $P_0 = 1 - \rho$ correctly but compute $\rho$ wrong as $\mu/\lambda$ instead of $\lambda/\mu$ (loses 1 mark).
> - **Valuation Tip:** Always state the units explicitly — "customers", "hours", "minutes" — to earn full marks.

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **scope and limitations of Operations Research** with at least three limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve the following **2x2 two-person zero-sum game** using the **mixed strategy method**:

|  | **B plays B1** | **B plays B2** |
|---|---|---|
| **A plays A1** | 4 | 1 |
| **A plays A2** | 2 | 5 |

Find the optimal strategies for both players and the value of the game. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Scope and Limitations of OR (7 marks):**

**Scope (3.5 marks):** OR has vast scope across domains — defense (logistics, missile trajectory), industry (production planning, inventory), finance (portfolio optimization), healthcare (hospital scheduling), transportation (vehicle routing), and public policy (electoral modeling). Module 4 tools (queuing, replacement, sequencing, game theory) extend OR into service operations, asset management, and strategic decision-making.

**Limitations (3.5 marks):**
1. **Human element quantification is difficult:** OR models struggle to capture human emotions, morale, and behavioral factors (e.g., worker resistance to change).
2. **Dependence on quantitative data:** OR is largely mathematical and cannot easily handle qualitative constraints like corporate culture or political environment.
3. **Cost of implementation:** Hiring OR experts, buying software, and training staff can be expensive for small organizations.
4. **Model validation challenges:** Real systems are dynamic; an OR model that worked last year may not work today.
5. **Black-box perception:** Managers may distrust mathematical outputs without understanding the model, leading to poor adoption.

---

**Part (b) — Mixed Strategy Game Solution (7 marks):**

**Payoff Matrix (Player A's gains):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1: Check for saddle point (1 mark).**

Row minima: $\min(4,1) = 1$, $\min(2,5) = 2$. Hence $\max_i \min_j = 2$.
Column maxima: $\max(4,2) = 4$, $\max(1,5) = 5$. Hence $\min_j \max_i = 4$.

Since $2 \neq 4$, **no saddle point exists**. Mixed strategy applies.

**Step 2: Set up probabilities (1 mark).**

Let Player A play A1 with probability $p$ and A2 with probability $(1-p)$.

**Step 3: Equate expected payoffs (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
4p + 2(1-p) &= 1p + 5(1-p) \\
4p + 2 - 2p &= p + 5 - 5p \\
2p + 2 &= -4p + 5 \\
6p &= 3 \\
p &= \dfrac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

So Player A plays A1 with probability $0.5$ and A2 with probability $0.5$.

**Step 4: Find Player B's optimal probabilities (2 marks).**

By symmetry (letting B play B1 with probability $q$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
4q + 1(1-q) &= 2q + 5(1-q) \\
4q + 1 - q &= 2q + 5 - 5q \\
3q + 1 &= -3q + 5 \\
6q &= 4 \\
q &= \dfrac{2}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

So Player B plays B1 with probability $2/3$ and B2 with probability $1/3$.

**Step 5: Compute the game value (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= 4 \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} + 2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,v = 3, \quad p = \tfrac{1}{2}, \quad q = \tfrac{2}{3}\,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Error 1:** Failing to verify the absence of a saddle point before applying mixed strategy (loses 1 mark).
> - **Common Error 2:** Sign errors when expanding $4p + 2(1-p)$ — students frequently forget to multiply the $1$ in $(1-p)$ by the second term (loses 1.5 marks).
> - **Common Error 3:** Not specifying that probabilities are in range $[0, 1]$; always validate $p, q \in [0,1]$ (loses 0.5 mark).
> - **Common Error 4:** Confusing "row minimum" vs. "column maximum" logic in saddle point test (loses 1 mark).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 4 (Importance and Applications)**

- **Definition:** OR = scientific method for optimal decision-making under constraints.
- **Six Phases of OR Study:** Formulation → Data → Model → Solution → Validation → Implementation.
- **Importance (Top 3 to memorize):** (1) Optimal resource allocation, (2) Quantifies trade-offs, (3) Handles complexity beyond human cognition.
- **M/M/1 Queue Key Results:** $L_s = \rho/(1-\rho)$, $L_q = \rho^2/(1-\rho)$, $P_0 = 1-\rho$, stability requires $\rho < 1$.
- **Replacement Models:** Two types — *individual* (item-by-item, expensive items) and *group* (bulk items, e.g., bulbs).
- **Sequencing Goal:** Minimize total makespan, idle time, or lateness — Johnson's rule for n-jobs/2-machines; extension for n/m machines.
- **Game Theory Saddle Point Condition:** $\max_i \min_j a_{ij} = \min_j \max_i a_{ij}$. If satisfied → pure strategy; else → mixed strategy.
- **Mixed Strategy Formula:** $p = (d-c)/((a+d)-(b+c))$, $v = (ad-bc)/((a+d)-(b+c))$ for 2x2 game.
- **Three Limitations to memorize:** (1) Human element hard to quantify, (2) Cost of expert implementation, (3) Dynamic systems may invalidate models.
- **Real-World Applications:** Queuing → banks, hospitals, call centers; Replacement → machinery, IT hardware; Sequencing → production lines; Game theory → bidding, defense, pricing.
- **Units to Always Write:** Customers, hours, minutes, ₹/year, dimensionless ratios — *never leave units implicit*.
- **Common Pitfall:** In M/M/1, if computed $\rho \geq 1$, the system is *unstable* — no steady state exists, and the model is invalid.

---

> **END OF NOTES — PEMET528 / Module 4 / Importance and Applications**
> *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | 2024 Scheme | Outcome-Based Education*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kendall's Notations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET528 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Kendall's Notations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kendall's Notation (1953)**: A standardized shorthand symbolic system proposed by **David G. Kendall** to classify and describe the structural characteristics of a **single waiting line (queue)** in queuing theory. The classical notation is expressed as a six-element tuple:

$$\text{Kendall's Notation} = \frac{A}{B} / c : K : N : D$$

Where each element represents a specific operational attribute of the queueing system. It is the universally accepted taxonomy used to identify queue models before applying analytical tools such as steady-state balance equations, Little's Law, or birth-death process formulations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Module 4 (Queuing Theory) requires students to first identify the queue using Kendall's notation *before* solving for performance measures (L, Lq, W, Wq, Pn, ρ). Marks are routinely awarded for correct identification of the model in **ESE (End Semester Examination)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a **Kerala State Road Transport Corporation (KSRTC) bus ticket counter at Thampanoor, Thiruvananthapuram** during a festival rush. To mathematically describe what is happening at that counter, an engineer would need to answer six questions:

1. **How do customers arrive?** — Randomly (M), at fixed intervals (D), or in bursts (Ek)?
2. **How long does each service take?** — Quick (Exponential), constant time (D), or variable (G)?
3. **How many counters are open?** — 1, 2, 5, ... (c)
4. **How many people can the hall accommodate?** — Infinite (∞) or limited (K)
5. **Where do customers come from?** — Unlimited city population (∞) or finite campus (N)?
6. **Who gets served next?** — First-Come-First-Served (FCFS), Last-Come-First-Served (LCFS), or Priority (PR)?

Kendall's notation is simply the **"ID card"** of the queue — it answers all six questions in a single compact code, allowing any researcher worldwide to immediately reconstruct and analyze the same system.

---

## 1.3 The Six Parameters — Quick Reference

> [!NOTE]
> **The Kendall Code** $\frac{A}{B} / c : K : N : D$ works like a postal address. Each "field" tells you something specific about the queue.

| Symbol | Meaning | Common Codes |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **A** | Arrival Process | **M**, D, Ek, Hk, G, GI |
| **B** | Service Time Distribution | **M**, D, Ek, Hk, G, GI |
| **c** | Number of Parallel Servers | 1, 2, 3, ..., ∞ |
| **K** | System Capacity (Waiting + Service) | Integer or **∞** |
| **N** | Calling Population Size | Integer or **∞** |
| **D** | Queue Discipline | **FCFS**, LCFS, SIRO, PR, GD |

- **Default Assumption**: If K, N, and D are omitted, they are assumed to be **∞**, **∞**, and **FCFS** respectively. This is the **Kendall's short notation**: $\frac{A}{B} / c$.
- **Memoryless Property**: The symbol **M** stands for **Markovian**, which mathematically corresponds to a **Poisson arrival process** and **exponential service time distribution**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Explanation of Each Parameter

### 2.1.1 The Arrival Process (A)

The arrival process describes the statistical pattern with which customers enter the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **inter-arrival time** $T_a$ is the time gap between two successive arrivals. Its probability distribution determines the symbol used.

- **M (Markovian)**: Inter-arrival times follow an **exponential distribution** with parameter $\lambda$ (mean arrival rate). This implies that arrivals follow a **Poisson process** $P_n(t) = \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$. Property: **memoryless** $P(T_a > s + t \mid T_a > s) = P(T_a > t)$.

- **D (Deterministic)**: Customers arrive at **fixed, exact intervals**. Inter-arrival time is a constant. Example: A train arriving precisely every 30 minutes.

- **$E_k$ (Erlang-k)**: Inter-arrival times follow an **Erlang distribution** with shape parameter $k$ and mean $\frac{1}{\lambda}$. Variance is $\frac{1}{k\lambda^2}$. This is a gamma distribution with integer shape — useful for modeling non-Poisson but non-deterministic arrivals.

- **$H_k$ (Hyperexponential)**: Inter-arrival times are **mixtures of exponentials**; variance > mean. Models highly variable arrival patterns.

- **G (General)**: Inter-arrival times follow an **arbitrary distribution** with mean $\frac{1}{\lambda}$ and variance $\sigma_a^2$. Only the first two moments are needed for analysis.

- **GI (General Independent)**: Same as G, but additionally requires that successive inter-arrival times be **statistically independent**.

### 2.1.2 The Service Time Distribution (B)

Same alphabet codes apply, but interpreted for the time taken by a server to complete one customer's service.

- **M**: Service times are exponentially distributed with parameter $\mu$ (mean service rate). Probability density function: $f(t) = \mu e^{-\mu t}$ for $t \geq 0$.
- **D**: Constant service time $T_s = \frac{1}{\mu}$.
- **Ek**: Erlang-k service with mean $\frac{1}{\mu}$ and variance $\frac{1}{k\mu^2}$.
- **G**: General service time distribution with mean $\frac{1}{\mu}$ and variance $\sigma_s^2$.

### 2.1.3 Number of Servers (c)

The integer $c \geq 1$ represents the count of **parallel service channels**. A multi-server queue may be visualized as $c$ identical service stations, each with its own queue (multi-channel, multi-phase) or one common queue feeding all servers (multi-channel, single-phase).

### 2.1.4 System Capacity (K)

The **maximum number of customers** that can be in the system at any instant (those being served + those waiting). When $K = \infty$, all arrivals eventually enter. When $K$ is finite, arriving customers who find the system full are **blocked/lost**.

### 2.1.5 Population Size (N)

The total number of **potential customers** that could potentially join the queue. $N = \infty$ corresponds to an infinite source (e.g., customers of a public bank). Finite $N$ is used for machine repair problems where the number of machines is limited.

### 2.1.6 Queue Discipline (D)

The rule that selects the next customer to be served:

- **FCFS** (First-Come-First-Served) — most natural, default.
- **LCFS** (Last-Come-First-Served) — elevators, inventory systems.
- **SIRO** (Service In Random Order) — lottery systems.
- **PR** (Priority) — emergency rooms.
- **GD** (General Discipline) — any rule, usually non-preemptive.

---

## 2.2 Why Kendall's Notation Matters — Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world KTU applications of correctly identifying the model**:
> - **Civil Engineering**: Traffic signal queues at junctions in Kochi city → typically modeled as M/M/1 with finite K.
> - **Computer Science**: Packet arrival at a router → M/M/1/K or M/G/1.
> - **Mechanical Engineering**: Machine breakdown-repair systems → M/M/c/N/N.
> - **Industrial Engineering**: KSRTC ticket counters, hospital OPD queues, ATM machines.

By pinpointing the model, engineers can apply the correct formulas for utilization ($\rho = \frac{\lambda}{c\mu}$), average queue length ($L_q$), and average waiting time ($W_q$) to optimize staffing, server allocation, and waiting space design.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates every standard KTU-asked parameter and formula tied to Kendall's notation identification.**

| Kendall Model | Traffic Intensity ($\rho$) | Steady-State Condition | Key Performance Formula |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $\frac{M}{M}{1}$ | $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | $\rho < 1$ | $L = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho},\ W = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ |
| $\frac{M}{M}{c}$ | $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{c\mu}$ | $\rho < 1$ | $P_0 = \left[\sum_{n=0}^{c-1} \frac{(c\rho)^n}{n!} + \frac{(c\rho)^c}{c!\,(1-\rho)}\right]^{-1}$ |
| $\frac{M}{M}{1}/K$ | $\rho \neq 1$ | Always stable | $P_0 = \frac{1 - \rho}{1 - \rho^{K+1}}$ |
| $\frac{M}{M}{c}/K$ | $\rho \neq 1$ | Always stable | $P_K = \frac{\rho^K}{\rho^K + \rho^K \cdot \text{term}}$ (use birth-death) |
| $\frac{M}{M}{1}/N/N$ | $\lambda_n = \lambda(N-n)$ | Always stable | Finite population, repair-machine models |
| $\frac{M}{G}{1}$ | $\rho = \lambda E[S]$ | $\rho < 1$ | $L_q = \frac{\rho^2 + \lambda^2 \sigma_s^2}{2(1-\rho)}$ (Pollaczek-Khinchine) |
| $\frac{M}{E_k}{1}$ | $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | $\rho < 1$ | $L_q = \frac{\rho^2 (k+1)}{2k(1-\rho)}$ |
| $\frac{M}{D}{1}$ | $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | $\rho < 1$ | $L_q = \frac{\rho^2}{2(1-\rho)}$ |
| $\frac{G}{G}{1}$ | $\rho = \lambda E[S]$ | $\rho < 1$ | Kingman's formula (approximation) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall**: Students often write $\frac{M}{M}{1}$ for a problem with 2 tellers at a bank. The correct notation is $\frac{M}{M}{2}$ — the third symbol is the **number of servers**, not the system capacity. Misidentifying this costs **2 marks minimum** in ESE.

---

## 2.4 The Underlying 'Why' — Birth-Death Process Link

Kendall's notation implicitly assumes the queue can be modeled as a **birth-death process** (when distributions are Markovian). The state $n$ = number of customers in the system.

- **Birth rate** $\lambda_n$: rate at which arrivals occur when system has $n$ customers.
- **Death rate** $\mu_n$: rate at which service completions occur.

In Kendall's standard **M/M/1** model:
$$\lambda_n = \lambda \quad \forall\, n \geq 0$$
$$\mu_n = \begin{cases} \mu, & n \geq 1 \\ 0, & n = 0 \end{cases}$$

The steady-state balance equation gives **geometric probabilities**:
$$P_n = (1 - \rho)\,\rho^n, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$
where $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$. This elegant form is the direct mathematical consequence of identifying the system as M/M/1 via Kendall's notation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: State Probabilities for M/M/1 from Kendall's Notation

We start by identifying the system as $\frac{M}{M}{1} : \infty : \infty : \text{FCFS}$ and progressively build the solution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1: Define the steady-state probability $P_n$** as the probability that exactly $n$ customers are in the system (in service + waiting) at an arbitrary instant in statistical equilibrium.

Let us consider an arbitrary state $n$ for $n \geq 1$. The flows in and out of this state must balance in the long run.

> **Step 2: Apply the steady-state (statistical equilibrium) flow balance at state $n$**:

The rate of entry into state $n$ equals the rate of exit from state $n$. The transition diagram in equilibrium is:
- **Up-transition** (from $n-1$ to $n$): occurs at rate $\lambda_{n-1} P_{n-1}$.
- **Down-transition** (from $n+1$ to $n$): occurs at rate $\mu_{n+1} P_{n+1}$.
- **Out from $n$**: at combined rate $(\lambda_n + \mu_n) P_n$.

Setting up-flow = down-flow:

$$\lambda_{n-1} P_{n-1} + \mu_{n+1} P_{n+1} = (\lambda_n + \mu_n) P_n$$

> **Step 3: Substitute M/M/1 parameters** ($\lambda_n = \lambda$, $\mu_n = \mu$ for $n \geq 1$):

$$\lambda P_{n-1} + \mu P_{n+1} = (\lambda + \mu) P_n$$

Rearranging:

$$\mu P_{n+1} - \mu P_n = \lambda P_n - \lambda P_{n-1}$$
$$\mu(P_{n+1} - P_n) = \lambda(P_n - P_{n-1})$$

> **Step 4: Define the ratio $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$** (traffic intensity / utilization factor):

$$P_{n+1} - P_n = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}(P_n - P_n) = \rho(P_n - P_{n-1})$$

> **Step 5: Telescoping sum** — Let $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ and observe that:

$$P_2 - P_1 = \rho(P_1 - P_0)$$
$$P_3 - P_2 = \rho(P_2 - P_1) = \rho^2(P_1 - P_0)$$
$$P_{n+1} - P_n = \rho^n(P_1 - P_0)$$

Summing from $i = 0$ to $i = n-1$:

$$P_n - P_0 = (P_1 - P_0)\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}\rho^i = (P_1 - P_0)\cdot\frac{1 - \rho^n}{1 - \rho}$$

> **Step 6: Apply the boundary condition at $n = 0$**:

In state 0, only arrivals occur (no service in progress). The flow balance for state 0:
$$\mu P_1 = \lambda P_0 \implies P_1 = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} P_0 = \rho P_0$$

Substituting $P_1 = \rho P_0$ into our telescoped expression:

$$P_n - P_0 = (\rho P_0 - P_0) \cdot \frac{1 - \rho^n}{1 - \rho}$$
$$P_n = P_0 + P_0(\rho - 1) \cdot \frac{1 - \rho^n}{1 - \rho}$$
$$P_n = P_0 - P_0(1 - \rho^n) = P_0(1 - 1 + \rho^n) = P_0\,\rho^n$$

> **Step 7: Apply the normalization condition** $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P_n = 1$:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P_0 \rho^n = P_0 \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \rho^n = P_0 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \rho} = 1 \quad \text{(for } \rho < 1\text{)}$$

Therefore:

$$P_0 = 1 - \rho$$

> **Step 8: Final closed-form expression for the M/M/1 queue**:

$$\boxed{P_n = (1 - \rho)\rho^n, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots \quad \text{where } \rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} < 1}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Expected Number in System (L) for M/M/1

Using the result from Step 8 above, we now derive $L = E[N]$:

$$L = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n P_n = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n (1-\rho) \rho^n = (1-\rho) \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \rho^n$$

Recall the standard infinite sum identity:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n x^n = \frac{x}{(1-x)^2} \quad \text{for } \vert x \vert < 1$$

Substituting $x = \rho$:

$$L = (1-\rho) \cdot \frac{\rho}{(1-\rho)^2} = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification via Little's Law**: $L = \lambda W$. With $W = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$, we get $L = \frac{\lambda}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho}$ since $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$. ✓ The identity is consistent — a strong indicator that our Kendall's notation identification was correct.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Classifying Common KTU Exam Problems

The following matrix is a **KTU-grade problem-classification tool**. By inspecting the problem text, the student should be able to map it to the correct Kendall code.

| Problem Wording Clue | Identified Parameter | Kendall's Code |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| "Customers arrive randomly at a rate of 10/hour" | $A = M$ (Poisson) | $\frac{M}{...}{...}$ |
| "Trucks arrive exactly every 15 minutes" | $A = D$ (deterministic) | $\frac{D}{...}{...}$ |
| "Service time follows exponential distribution" | $B = M$ | $\frac{M}{M}{...}$ |
| "Average service time is constant at 5 minutes" | $B = D$ | $\frac{M}{D}{...}$ |
| "There is a single billing counter" | $c = 1$ | $\frac{M}{M}{1}$ |
| "Two tellers serve from one common queue" | $c = 2$ | $\frac{M}{M}{2}$ |
| "The waiting room can hold at most 20 people" | $K = 20$ | $\frac{M}{M}{1}/20$ |
| "Arriving customers leave if no place to stand" | $K$ finite (loss system) | $\frac{M}{M}{1}/K$ |
| "5 machines, 1 repairman" | $N = 5,\ c = 1$ | $\frac{M}{M}{1}/N/5$ |
| "Patients are seen in order of arrival" | $D = \text{FCFS}$ | $\frac{M}{M}{1}$ (default) |
| "Emergency patients are seen first" | $D = \text{PR}$ | $\frac{M}{M}{1}:\infty:\infty:\text{PR}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Strategy Tip**: In an ESE, after reading the problem, always **write the full Kendall's notation explicitly** at the top of your solution. This alone may earn you 1-2 marks and signals to the examiner that you understand the model.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Complete Identification

**Problem**: At a KSRTC reservation counter, customers arrive at a Poisson rate of 12 per hour. Service times are exponentially distributed with mean 4 minutes. There is one server and unlimited queue space. Customers are served in FCFS order.

**Solution (Identification step)**:
- $A = M$ (Poisson arrival) → first symbol
- $B = M$ (exponential service) → second symbol
- $c = 1$ (single server) → third symbol
- $K = \infty$ (default) → omitted
- $N = \infty$ (default) → omitted
- $D = \text{FCFS}$ (default) → omitted

$$\text{Kendall's Notation: } \frac{M}{M}/1$$

**Quick computation**:
- $\lambda = 12/\text{hr}$, $\mu = \frac{60}{4} = 15/\text{hr}$
- $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{12}{15} = 0.8$ ✓ (steady state satisfied)
- $L = \frac{0.8}{1-0.8} = 4$ customers
- $L_q = L - \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = 4 - 0.8 = 3.2$ customers
- $W = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{1}{3}\text{ hr} = 20\text{ min}$
- $W_q = W - \frac{1}{\mu} = 20 - 4 = 16\text{ min}$

> This single example integrates **identification (Kendall) + analysis (formulas)** — the exact pattern KTU examiners reward.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Kendall's Notation — Decoding Map

The following Mermaid flowchart decodes each symbol of the Kendall's Notation tuple $\frac{A}{B}/c:K:N:D$ and shows how problem text translates into the formal code.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read Queuing Problem Statement] --> B{How do customers arrive?}
    B -->|Random Poisson| B1[A = M Markovian]
    B -->|Fixed interval| B2[A = D Deterministic]
    B -->|Erlang pattern| B3[A = Ek Erlang-k]
    B -->|Arbitrary pattern| B4[A = G or GI General]

    B1 --> C{How is service time distributed?}
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C

    C -->|Exponential| C1[B = M Markovian]
    C -->|Constant| C2[B = D Deterministic]
    C -->|Erlang| C3[B = Ek Erlang-k]
    C -->|Arbitrary| C4[B = G General]

    C1 --> D{How many parallel servers?}
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D

    D -->|Single| D1[c = 1]
    D -->|Multiple| D2[c = n greater than 1]

    D1 --> E{System capacity finite?}
    D2 --> E

    E -->|Yes| E1[K = integer]
    E -->|No| E2[K = infinity default]

    E1 --> F{Calling population size}
    E2 --> F

    F -->|Unlimited| F1[N = infinity default]
    F -->|Finite machines| F2[N = integer]

    F1 --> G{Service discipline}
    F2 --> G

    G -->|First come| G1[D = FCFS default]
    G -->|Priority| G2[D = PR]
    G -->|Random| G3[D = SIRO]

    G1 --> H[Final Kendall Code A slash B slash c colon K colon N colon D]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading this diagram**: Begin at the top with the problem text. Each diamond is a **decision node** asking one of the six questions. Each rectangular box represents the **symbol assigned** for that parameter. The final node `H` shows the assembled Kendall code.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Queue as a System

The following diagram depicts the **internal architecture** of a queueing system, mapping each Kendall parameter to its functional block.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Calling Population of size N] -->|Arrival rate lambda| A[Arrival Process A]
    A -->|Inter arrival time T_a| GATE{Capacity check K}
    GATE -->|Full blocked or lost| X[Customer exits system]
    GATE -->|Space available| Q[Waiting Line of size up to K]
    Q -->|Discipline D| S[Service Facility with c parallel servers]
    S -->|Service time T_s exponential mean one over mu| OUT[Departure]

    S -->|Server count c| C[Server Block c equals 1, 2, 3...]
    A -->|Statistical law M D Ek G| AP[Arrival Distribution Module]
    S -->|Service law M D Ek G| SP[Service Distribution Module]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation**:
> - **SRC → A**: Population feeds the arrival process (the first Kendall field).
> - **GATE**: Implements the system capacity $K$ — customers rejected if full.
> - **Q**: Waiting line; size depends on $\lambda$, $\mu$, and $c$.
> - **S**: Service facility with $c$ parallel servers, time distribution $B$.
> - **D**: Discipline that selects the next customer to be served.

---

## 4.3 Parameter Hierarchy Diagram

The relationship between the six Kendall parameters in a hierarchical dependency:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Kendall Code A over B over c colon K colon N colon D] --> P1[Layer 1: Arrival A]
    P0 --> P2[Layer 1: Service B]
    P0 --> P3[Layer 1: Server Count c]
    P1 --> P11[Sets lambda]
    P2 --> P21[Sets mu]
    P11 --> P12[Defines rho = lambda over mu]
    P21 --> P12
    P3 --> P31[Modifies effective service rate c mu]
    P31 --> P12
    P12 --> P4[Layer 2: Performance metrics L W Lq Wq]
    P0 --> P5[Layer 2: Capacity K bounds state space]
    P0 --> P6[Layer 3: Population N limits source]
    P0 --> P7[Layer 3: Discipline D affects waiting order]
    P5 --> P4
    P6 --> P4
    P7 --> P4
```

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram clarifies the **causal flow** in Kendall's framework: the arrival distribution, service distribution, and server count together determine the traffic intensity $\rho$ and consequently all steady-state performance measures.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern**: Part A questions test **recall (Bloom Level 1) and basic understanding (Bloom Level 2)**. Answers should be 4-6 lines with a clean definition plus example.

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

> **"What is Kendall's notation? Explain the symbols M, D, Ek used in describing a queueing system."**

**Model Answer**:

Kendall's notation is a **standardized symbolic representation** proposed by D.G. Kendall (1953) to classify queuing systems. It is written as a six-tuple:

$$\frac{A}{B}/c : K : N : D$$

where $A$ = arrival process, $B$ = service time distribution, $c$ = number of servers, $K$ = system capacity, $N$ = population size, and $D$ = queue discipline.

The three common symbols are:

- **M (Markovian)**: Inter-arrival or service times follow an **exponential distribution**. For arrivals, this implies a Poisson process. Example: Random customer arrivals at a counter.
- **D (Deterministic)**: Inter-arrival or service times are **constant / fixed**. Example: A bus arriving every 30 minutes.
- **$E_k$ (Erlang-k)**: Inter-arrival or service times follow an **Erlang distribution** with shape parameter $k$. Example: A telecommunications call with $k$ sequential phases.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

> **"Write the Kendall's notation for an M/M/2 queueing system with finite capacity 5, finite population 20, and priority discipline. List all six parameters."**

**Model Answer**:

For the given system, the Kendall's notation is:

$$\frac{M}{M}/2 : 5 : 20 : \text{PR}$$

Listing all six parameters:

| Position | Symbol | Meaning |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1 | $A = M$ | Arrivals are **Markovian** (Poisson) |
| 2 | $B = M$ | Service times are **Markovian** (exponential) |
| 3 | $c = 2$ | Two parallel servers |
| 4 | $K = 5$ | Maximum **5** customers in system |
| 5 | $N = 20$ | Calling population of **20** |
| 6 | $D = \text{PR}$ | **Priority** service discipline |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern (Module 4 ESE)**: Part B questions carry **14 marks**, usually with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must attempt **either** Option A **or** Option B in full. We present two independent alternatives below.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, Adapted]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] Define Kendall's notation. Explain any **five symbols** used in the notation with a suitable engineering example for each.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Customers arrive at a single-server KSRTC booking counter according to a Poisson process with rate $\lambda = 8$ per hour. Service times follow an exponential distribution with mean 6 minutes. **Identify the model using Kendall's notation** and compute:
> (i) Traffic intensity $\rho$
> (ii) Probability that the system is idle ($P_0$)
> (iii) Expected number in the system ($L$) and queue ($L_q$)
> (iv) Expected waiting time in the system ($W$) and queue ($W_q$)

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Definition of Kendall's notation** [3 of 7 marks]:

Kendall's notation is a compact six-symbol representation $\frac{A}{B}/c:K:N:D$ used to fully specify a queueing system. Originally proposed by D.G. Kendall in 1953, it standardizes communication about queue models among researchers and engineers.

**Five symbols with engineering examples** [4 of 7 marks]:

1. **M (Markovian)** — Used when arrival or service times are exponentially distributed. Example: Random breakdown arrivals at a repair shop in an industrial plant.
2. **D (Deterministic)** — Fixed inter-arrival or service time. Example: A metro train arriving at Kochi Metro station every 10 minutes.
3. **c (Number of servers)** — Parallel service channels. Example: $c = 3$ tells us there are 3 counters at a bank.
4. **K (System capacity)** — Maximum permissible customers. Example: A 50-seater waiting hall at a hospital OPD has $K = 50$.
5. **D (Discipline)** — Service order rule. Example: Emergency patients in a hospital use PR (priority) discipline.

**(b) Solution** [7 marks]:

**Step 1 — Model Identification using Kendall's Notation** [1 mark]:

- Arrivals: Poisson → $A = M$
- Service: Exponential → $B = M$
- Single server → $c = 1$
- Defaults: $K = \infty,\ N = \infty,\ D = \text{FCFS}$

$$\boxed{\text{Model: } \frac{M}{M}/1}$$

**Step 2 — Parameter conversion** [1 mark]:

- $\lambda = 8/\text{hr}$
- Mean service time $= 6$ min $= \frac{6}{60} = 0.1$ hr
- $\mu = \frac{1}{0.1} = 10/\text{hr}$

**Step 3 — Compute Traffic Intensity** [1 mark]:

$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8$$

Since $\rho = 0.8 < 1$, **steady state exists**. ✓

**Step 4 — Probability System is Idle** [1 mark]:

$$P_0 = 1 - \rho = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2$$

**Step 5 — Expected Number in System $L$ and Queue $L_q$** [2 marks]:

$$L = \frac{\rho}{1 - \rho} = \frac{0.8}{1 - 0.8} = \frac{0.8}{0.2} = 4 \text{ customers}$$

$$L_q = L - \rho = 4 - 0.8 = 3.2 \text{ customers}$$

**Step 6 — Expected Time in System $W$ and Queue $W_q$** [1 mark]:

$$W = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{1}{10 - 8} = \frac{1}{2} \text{ hr} = 30 \text{ minutes}$$

$$W_q = W - \frac{1}{\mu} = 30 - 6 = 24 \text{ minutes}$$

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4, Adapted]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] Compare the **Kendall's notation systems** for the following queue models, clearly stating the assumptions for each:
> (i) M/M/1
> (ii) M/M/1/K
> (iii) M/M/c/N/N
> (iv) M/G/1
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] At a tool crib of a manufacturing workshop, 4 machines are served by 1 mechanic. Machine running time (time to failure) is exponential with mean 80 hours. Repair time is exponential with mean 6 hours. **Identify the model in Kendall's notation** and find:
> (i) Effective arrival rate $\lambda_{\text{eff}}$
> (ii) Average number of machines waiting for repair, $L_q$
> (iii) Probability that the mechanic is idle

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Comparison of Queue Models** [7 marks]:

| Model | Kendall Code | Assumptions | Engineering Use |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| (i) M/M/1 | $\frac{M}{M}/1$ | Poisson arrival, exponential service, 1 server, $K=\infty$, $N=\infty$, FCFS | Simple counter, single ATM |
| (ii) M/M/1/K | $\frac{M}{M}/1:K$ | As above but **finite system capacity** $K$; arrivals blocked if full | Parking lot, telephone trunk with limited lines |
| (iii) M/M/c/N/N | $\frac{M}{M}/c:N:N$ | $c$ servers, **finite population** of size $N$, finite source alters arrival rate $\lambda_n = \lambda(N-n)$ | **Machine repair problem** (this exact problem!) |
| (iv) M/G/1 | $\frac{M}{G}/1$ | Poisson arrival, **general service distribution**, 1 server | Real-world systems where service time is not exponential |

**(b) Solution to Machine Repair Problem** [7 marks]:

**Step 1 — Model Identification** [1 mark]:

- Poisson-like arrival of failures → $A = M$
- Exponential repair time → $B = M$
- 1 mechanic → $c = 1$
- 4 machines in population → $N = 4$
- System capacity $= N = 4$ (at most all machines broken) → $K = 4$
- Default discipline → $D = \text{FCFS}$

$$\boxed{\text{Model: } \frac{M}{M}/1/4/4 \text{ or simply } M/M/1/N/N}$$

**Step 2 — Parameters** [1 mark]:

- $\lambda = \frac{1}{80}$ per hour (failure rate of one machine)
- $\mu = \frac{1}{6}$ per hour (repair rate)
- $N = 4$

**Step 3 — Effective Arrival Rate** [1 mark]:

Since the source is finite, the rate depends on the number of operational machines. In equilibrium, the mean effective arrival rate is:

$$\lambda_{\text{eff}} = \mu\,(L - L_q) = \mu(N - L) = \mu \cdot 4(1 - P_0)\cdot \text{(varies)}$$

Alternatively, the standard formula for M/M/1/N/N with $N = 4$:

$$\lambda_{\text{eff}} = \lambda (N - L) = \frac{1}{80}(4 - L)$$

This implicit equation is solved numerically in the next step.

**Step 4 — Probability Mechanic is Idle** [2 marks]:

For M/M/1/N/N, the state probabilities are:

$$P_n = \frac{N!}{n!(N-n)!}\left(\frac{\lambda}{\mu}\right)^n P_0$$

Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{1/80}{1/6} = \frac{6}{80} = 0.075$$

Let $r = 0.075$. The binomial expansion:

$$P_0 = \left[\sum_{n=0}^{4} \binom{4}{n} r^n\right]^{-1} = \left[\binom{4}{0} + \binom{4}{1} r + \binom{4}{2} r^2 + \binom{4}{3} r^3 + \binom{4}{4} r^4\right]^{-1}$$

Compute each term:
- $\binom{4}{0} = 1$
- $\binom{4}{1} r = 4 \times 0.075 = 0.300$
- $\binom{4}{2} r^2 = 6 \times 0.005625 = 0.03375$
- $\binom{4}{3} r^3 = 4 \times 0.000422 = 0.001687$
- $\binom{4}{4} r^4 = 1 \times 0.0000316 = 0.0000316$

Sum: $1 + 0.300 + 0.03375 + 0.001687 + 0.0000316 = 1.33547$

Therefore:

$$P_0 = \frac{1}{1.33547} \approx 0.7488$$

So the **mechanic is idle with probability ≈ 0.7488 (74.88%)**. ✓

**Step 5 — Average Number Waiting $L_q$** [2 marks]:

$$L_q = \sum_{n=1}^{N} (n-1) P_n = N - \frac{\lambda_{\text{eff}} + \mu}{\lambda}$$

Compute $\lambda_{\text{eff}}$:

$$L = \sum_{n=0}^{4} n P_n = 0 \cdot P_0 + 1 \cdot P_1 + 2 \cdot P_2 + 3 \cdot P_3 + 4 \cdot P_4$$

Using $P_n = \binom{4}{n}(0.075)^n P_0$:

$$P_1 = 4 \times 0.075 \times 0.7488 = 0.2246$$
$$P_2 = 6 \times 0.005625 \times 0.7488 = 0.02527$$
$$P_3 = 4 \times 0.000422 \times 0.7488 = 0.001264$$
$$P_4 = 1 \times 0.0000316 \times 0.7488 = 0.0000237$$

$$L = 1(0.2246) + 2(0.02527) + 3(0.001264) + 4(0.0000237) \approx 0.2775$$

Then:

$$L_q = L - (1 - P_0) = 0.2775 - 0.2512 \approx 0.026 \text{ machines}$$

This means on average, only a tiny fraction of a machine is waiting for repair — the mechanic is over-staffed for this workload.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Kendall's Notation Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme)**:
>
> 1. **Confusing $c$ with $K$**: $c$ is the **number of servers** (third position), $K$ is the **system capacity** (fourth position). Writing $\frac{M}{M}{K}/1$ instead of $\frac{M}{M}{1}/K$ loses **2-3 marks**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to specify the model before computing**: Always write the **complete Kendall's notation** at the top of your answer. Examiners reward this as a separate "identification" sub-step.
>
> 3. **Unit mismatch**: Converting $\lambda$ and $\mu$ to the **same time unit** is mandatory. A common error is mixing "per hour" arrival with "per minute" service rate. **Always convert first, then compute $\rho$.**
>
> 4. **Omitting verification of $\rho < 1$**: KTU examiners expect you to state explicitly that the system is in steady state. If $\rho \geq 1$, the system is unstable and the standard formulas do not apply.
>
> 5. **Mixing up $L_q$ and $L$**: $L$ = average number **in the system**; $L_q$ = average number **in the queue** (waiting, not being served). $L = L_q + \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$.
>
> 6. **Skipping the Little's Law check**: Always verify $L = \lambda W$ and $L_q = \lambda W_q$ to catch arithmetic errors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Kendall's Notation (Module 4)**:

- **Kendall's notation** is the **standardized 6-tuple** $\frac{A}{B}/c:K:N:D$ for classifying a queue.
- **Originator**: D.G. Kendall, **1953**.
- **Six parameters**: Arrival process ($A$), Service time distribution ($B$), Number of servers ($c$), System capacity ($K$), Population size ($N$), Service discipline ($D$).
- **M = Markovian** (memoryless exponential / Poisson); **D = Deterministic**; **$E_k$ = Erlang-k**; **$H_k$ = Hyperexponential**; **G = General**; **GI = General Independent**.
- **Default values** (when omitted): $K = \infty,\ N = \infty,\ D = \text{FCFS}$ — these are **Kendall's short notation** $\frac{A}{B}/c$.
- **Most common KTU model**: $\frac{M}{M}/1$ (Poisson arrival, exponential service, single server, infinite capacity).
- **Birth-death linkage**: $P_n$ satisfies the detailed balance equation $\lambda_{n-1} P_{n-1} + \mu_{n+1} P_{n+1} = (\lambda_n + \mu_n) P_n$.
- **Traffic intensity** $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{c\mu}$ — must be $< 1$ for steady state in M/M/1.
- **Performance measures always computed**: $L, L_q, W, W_q, P_0, P_n$ — using Little's Law $L = \lambda W$.
- **Finite vs Infinite**: The K, N, D parameters transform the basic M/M/1 model into specialized variants for engineering applications (machine repair, lost-call systems, priority queues).
- **Real-world KTU examples**: KSRTC counters, hospital OPD, machine repair shops, ATM queues, telephone exchanges, traffic junctions.
- **Always write the full Kendall's notation** at the start of every queuing problem in ESE — it earns marks and demonstrates model awareness.
- **Verification trick**: Use Little's Law ($L = \lambda W$, $L_q = \lambda W_q$) to catch calculation errors in $\rho, L, L_q, W, W_q$.
- **Pollaczek-Khinchine formula** for M/G/1: $L_q = \frac{\rho^2 + \lambda^2 \sigma_s^2}{2(1-\rho)}$ — high-yield for KTU Module 4 Part B questions.
- **Kingman's approximation** for G/G/1: $W_q \approx \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} \cdot \frac{C_a^2 + C_s^2}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\mu}$ where $C_a, C_s$ are coefficients of variation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of Queuing Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET528 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Queuing Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 – Characteristics of Queuing Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

A **Queuing System** is a mathematical abstraction used to model the flow of entities (customers, jobs, packets, vehicles, or raw materials) through a service facility where arrival and service times are stochastic in nature. The **Characteristics of a Queuing System** refer to the defining structural and behavioral parameters that completely describe how customers arrive, wait, get served, and depart from a system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A queuing system is characterized by six fundamental attributes: (1) the calling population, (2) the arrival pattern, (3) the queue discipline, (4) the service mechanism, (5) the number of service channels, and (6) the system capacity. Together, these attributes uniquely determine the performance behavior of the system.

The standard compact descriptor for any queuing system is **Kendall's Notation**, written as:

$$(a/b/c):(d/e/f)$$

where each letter represents a specific characteristic, and the final three are often omitted when they take default values (infinite capacity, single phase, FIFO discipline).

### The Six Core Characteristics

| # | Characteristic | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Calling Population** | The set of potential customers (finite or infinite) |
| 2 | **Arrival Pattern** | Statistical distribution governing inter-arrival times |
| 3 | **Queue Discipline** | The rule used to select the next customer for service |
| 4 | **Service Mechanism** | The number of servers and the service time distribution |
| 5 | **System Capacity** | The maximum number of customers allowed in the system |
| 6 | **Number of Service Phases** | Whether service consists of one or more sequential stages |

### Conceptual Analogy — The Bank Counter

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check (Bank Teller Counter):**
> Imagine walking into a bank. People keep arriving at irregular intervals (this is the **arrival pattern**). There is a single queue leading to three service windows (**three servers**). When a window becomes free, the person at the front of the queue moves forward (**queue discipline = FIFO**). Each person takes a variable amount of time to complete their transaction (**service time**). If the bank is small, only 30 people can stand inside (**finite capacity**) — the 31st person is turned away. This entire real-world scene is a queuing system, and the six characteristics above are the "ingredients" we identify to analyze it.

### Common Symbols in KTU Textbook Notation

- **λ (lambda)** = Mean arrival rate (customers per unit time)
- **μ (mu)** = Mean service rate (customers per unit time per server)
- **ρ (rho)** = Traffic intensity / server utilization factor
- **P_n** = Steady-state probability of exactly $n$ customers in the system
- **L** = Expected number of customers in the system
- **L_q** = Expected number of customers in the queue (excluding the one in service)
- **W** = Expected waiting time in the system
- **W_q** = Expected waiting time in the queue
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Examination of Each Characteristic

### A. Calling Population (Source)

The calling population is the pool from which customers originate. It is classified as:

- **Infinite Population**: The number of potential customers is so large that arrivals occur at a constant average rate $\lambda$ regardless of how many are already in the system. Example: calls arriving at a telephone exchange.
- **Finite Population**: A small, identifiable set of potential customers where each arrival reduces the source pool. Example: a workshop with 5 machines that may break down (a broken machine is unavailable to break again until repaired).

### B. Arrival Pattern

The arrival pattern describes how customers enter the system. It is characterized by the inter-arrival time distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Poisson Arrivals (Markovian):** When arrivals occur completely at random with average rate $\lambda$, the number of arrivals in time $t$ follows a Poisson distribution, and inter-arrival times follow an **exponential distribution**. This is the most common and tractable assumption in KTU problems. Denoted by the letter **M** in Kendall's notation.

Other arrival patterns:
- **Deterministic (D):** Arrivals occur at fixed, equal intervals (e.g., a bus every 30 minutes).
- **General (G):** Inter-arrival times follow an arbitrary distribution.
- **Erlian ($E_k$):** Inter-arrival times follow an Erlang distribution of order $k$.

### C. Queue Discipline

The queue discipline specifies the order in which waiting customers are selected for service.

| Discipline | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **FIFO** (First-In-First-Out) | Customers served in order of arrival | Bank queue, supermarket |
| **LIFO** (Last-In-First-Out) | Last arrival served first | Stack of plates, elevator loading |
| **SIRO** (Service-In-Random-Order) | Next customer picked randomly | Hospital emergency |
| **PR** (Priority) | Customers with higher precedence served first | VIP passengers, ICU patients |
| **RR** (Round Robin) | Each customer gets a fixed time quantum | CPU process scheduling |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module 4 problems **always assume FIFO** unless explicitly stated otherwise.

### D. Service Mechanism

The service mechanism has two components:

1. **Number of Servers (channels)** — single channel ($c = 1$) or multi-channel ($c = m$).
2. **Service Time Distribution** — usually **exponential** (memoryless) with mean service time $\frac{1}{\mu}$ and service rate $\mu$ per server.

### E. System Capacity

- **Infinite Capacity ($K = \infty$):** No upper limit on the number of customers in the system. Default assumption.
- **Finite Capacity ($K$):** When the system is full, new arrivals are **blocked/lost** (blocked-customer-cleared discipline).

### F. Number of Service Phases

- **Single Phase:** Service completed in one step.
- **Multi-Phase:** Customer moves through a series of stations (e.g., registration $\rightarrow$ examination $\rightarrow$ billing at a hospital).

## 2.2 Kendall's Notation — The Compact Identifier

$$(a/b/c):(d/e/f)$$

| Position | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $a$ | M, D, G, $E_k$ | Arrival distribution |
| $b$ | M, D, G, $E_k$ | Service time distribution |
| $c$ | $1, 2, \ldots, m$ | Number of parallel servers |
| $d$ | $1, 2, \ldots$ | Number of service phases (default = 1) |
| $e$ | $1, 2, \ldots$ | System capacity (default = $\infty$) |
| $f$ | FIFO, LIFO, SIRO, PR | Queue discipline (default = FIFO) |

**Common KTU Examples:**
- $(M/M/1):(FIFO/\infty)$ — Single server, exponential arrivals and services
- $(M/M/c):(FIFO/\infty)$ — Multi-server with $c$ channels
- $(M/M/1/K):(FIFO/K)$ — Single server with finite capacity $K$
- $(M/M/c/K/K):(FIFO)$ — Machine repair problem (finite population of $K$ machines)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — M/M/1 and M/M/c Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **high-frequency KTU exam items**. Memorize the steady-state relationships and the meaning of every variable.

### M/M/1 System (Infinite Capacity, Single Server, FIFO)

| Parameter | Formula | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Traffic Intensity | $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | $\rho < 1$ for steady state |
| $P_0$ (system empty) | $P_0 = 1 - \rho$ | $0 \le P_0 \le 1$ |
| $P_n$ ($n$ customers) | $P_n = (1 - \rho)\,\rho^{n}$ | $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ |
| Expected number in system | $L = \dfrac{\rho}{1 - \rho} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu - \lambda}$ | Finite only if $\rho < 1$ |
| Expected number in queue | $L_q = \dfrac{\rho^{2}}{1 - \rho}$ | Excludes customer in service |
| Expected time in system | $W = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ | By Little's Law |
| Expected waiting time in queue | $W_q = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu(1 - \rho)} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)}$ | Critical exam variable |

### M/M/c System (Multi-Server, Infinite Capacity)

| Parameter | Formula | Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Offered Load | $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ (per server utilization) | $\rho / c < 1$ |
| $P_0$ | $P_0 = \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{c-1} \dfrac{\rho^{n}}{n!} + \dfrac{\rho^{c}}{c!\,(1 - \rho/c)} \right]^{-1}$ | $\lambda \vert c\mu$ |
| $L_q$ | $L_q = \dfrac{P_0 \,\rho^{c}\,\rho}{c!\,(c - \rho)^{2}}$ | — |
| $L$ | $L = L_q + \rho$ | Little's Law |
| $W_q$ | $W_q = \dfrac{L_q}{\lambda}$ | Little's Law |
| $W$ | $W = W_q + \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ | Little's Law |

### Little's Law (Universal Identity)

$$L = \lambda W \qquad L_q = \lambda W_q$$

This is the most powerful relationship in queuing theory — it applies to **virtually any system** in steady state.

## 2.4 Transient vs. Steady-State Behavior

- **Transient State:** The system is just starting; probabilities are time-dependent.
- **Steady State:** The system has been running long enough that the probability distribution $P_n(t)$ becomes independent of time $t$. KTU Module 4 analysis is **always steady-state**.

> [!WARNING]
> The condition $\rho < 1$ (or $\lambda < c\mu$) is **mandatory** for a steady state to exist. If $\lambda \geq \mu$, the queue grows indefinitely and the system is unstable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Probabilities for M/M/1

The standard approach is to use a **birth-death process** representation. The states are $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ representing the number of customers in the system.

### Step 1: Define the Transition Rates

- **Birth rate** (arrival) at state $n$: $\lambda_n = \lambda$ (constant, because infinite population)
- **Death rate** (service completion) at state $n$: $\mu_n = \mu$ for $n \geq 1$, and $\mu_0 = 0$ (no service when system is empty)

### Step 2: Write the Balance Equations

In steady state, the rate of flow into each state equals the rate of flow out:

$$\lambda_{n-1}\,P_{n-1} + \mu_{n+1}\,P_{n+1} = (\lambda_n + \mu_n)\,P_n, \quad n \geq 1$$

For state $n = 0$, the balance is:

$$\mu_1\,P_1 = \lambda_0\,P_0$$

### Step 3: Apply the Detailed Balance for Birth-Death Chains

For a birth-death process, the detailed balance holds between adjacent states:

$$\lambda_{n-1}\,P_{n-1} = \mu_n\,P_n$$

### Step 4: Solve Recursively

**For $n = 1$:**
$$\lambda_0\,P_0 = \mu_1\,P_1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; P_1 = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}\,P_0 = \rho\,P_0$$

**For $n = 2$:**
$$\lambda_1\,P_1 = \mu_2\,P_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; P_2 = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}\,P_1 = \rho^{2}\,P_0$$

**Inductively for general $n$:**
$$P_n = \rho^{n}\,P_0$$

### Step 5: Apply the Normalization Condition

All probabilities must sum to unity:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P_n = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; P_0 \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \rho^{n} = 1$$

This is a geometric series valid only when $\rho < 1$:

$$P_0 \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 - \rho} = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; P_0 = 1 - \rho$$

### Step 6: Final Form of the Steady-State Distribution

$$P_n = (1 - \rho)\,\rho^{n}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Expected Number in the System $L$

Starting from the steady-state distribution and using the definition of expectation:

$$L = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n\,P_n = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n\,(1 - \rho)\,\rho^{n} = (1 - \rho)\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n\,\rho^{n}$$

Using the standard result for the sum of a weighted geometric series:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n\,x^{n} = \dfrac{x}{(1 - x)^{2}}, \quad \text{for } \vert x \vert < 1$$

Substitute $x = \rho$:

$$L = (1 - \rho) \cdot \dfrac{\rho}{(1 - \rho)^{2}} = \dfrac{\rho}{1 - \rho}$$

This can be written in terms of $\lambda$ and $\mu$ as:

$$L = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu - \lambda}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Expected Waiting Time in Queue $W_q$

The expected waiting time **in the queue** is the time until service begins (not including service time). From the standard result:

$$L_q = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} (n - 1)\,P_n = L - (1 - P_0) = L - \rho$$

Therefore:

$$L_q = \dfrac{\rho}{1 - \rho} - \rho = \dfrac{\rho^{2}}{1 - \rho}$$

Apply **Little's Law** to the queue:

$$W_q = \dfrac{L_q}{\lambda} = \dfrac{\rho^{2}}{\lambda(1 - \rho)} = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu(1 - \rho)}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — A KTU-Style Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** Customers arrive at a single-server ATM booth at the rate of 10 per hour. The service time averages 4 minutes. Assuming Poisson arrivals and exponential service times, determine the steady-state values of $P_0, P_1, L, L_q, W, W_q$ and the probability that a customer has to wait for service.

**Given:** $\lambda = 10$/hr, mean service time $= 4$ min $= \dfrac{1}{15}$ hr, so $\mu = 15$/hr.

**Step 1: Compute the traffic intensity**
$$\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu} = \dfrac{10}{15} = \dfrac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667$$

**Step 2: Compute $P_0$**
$$P_0 = 1 - \rho = 1 - \dfrac{2}{3} = \dfrac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333$$

**Step 3: Compute $P_1$**
$$P_1 = (1 - \rho)\,\rho = \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} = \dfrac{2}{9} \approx 0.2222$$

**Step 4: Expected number in system**
$$L = \dfrac{\rho}{1 - \rho} = \dfrac{2/3}{1/3} = 2 \text{ customers}$$

**Step 5: Expected number in queue**
$$L_q = \dfrac{\rho^{2}}{1 - \rho} = \dfrac{4/9}{1/3} = \dfrac{4}{3} \approx 1.3333 \text{ customers}$$

**Step 6: Expected time in system**
$$W = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \dfrac{1}{15 - 10} = \dfrac{1}{5}\text{ hr} = 12 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 7: Expected waiting time in queue**
$$W_q = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)} = \dfrac{10}{15 \times 5} = \dfrac{2}{15}\text{ hr} = 8 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 8: Probability that a customer has to wait**

A customer must wait if at least one customer is already being served when they arrive, which corresponds to $n \geq 1$:

$$P(\text{wait}) = 1 - P_0 = 1 - \dfrac{1}{3} = \dfrac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667$$

**Interpretation:** The server is busy two-thirds of the time, and a freshly arriving customer spends an average of 8 minutes in the queue.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic Queuing System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Calling Population Source] -->|arrivals at rate lambda| arr[Arrival Process]
    arr --> q{Queue}
    q -->|waits in line| serv[Service Facility with c servers]
    serv -->|service rate mu per server| out[Departures to Outside World]
    serv -.feedback.-> q
    style src fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style arr fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style q fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style serv fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style out fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Birth-Death State Transition Diagram (M/M/1)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s0((State 0)) -->|lambda| s1((State 1))
    s1 -->|lambda| s2((State 2))
    s2 -->|lambda| s3((State 3))
    s3 -->|lambda| s4((State n))
    s4 -->|lambda| s5((...))
    s1 -->|mu| s0
    s2 -->|mu| s1
    s3 -->|mu| s2
    s4 -->|mu| s3
    s5 -->|mu| s4
    style s0 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style s1 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style s2 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style s3 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style s4 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style s5 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each circle represents a system state (number of customers). Horizontal arrows upward are arrivals (births) at rate $\lambda$; arrows downward are service completions (deaths) at rate $\mu$. This is the fundamental Markov chain that powers M/M/1 analysis.

## 4.3 Multi-Server (M/M/c) Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Customer Arrival at rate lambda] --> B{Single Common Queue}
    B --> C1[Server 1 with rate mu]
    B --> C2[Server 2 with rate mu]
    B --> C3[Server c with rate mu]
    C1 --> D[Departure at rate c times mu]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D

    style B fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    style C2 fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C3 fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

```mermaid

    K --> A2[Service Time Distribution: M, D, G, Ek]
```
