<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define a queuing system and list any four characteristics of a queuing system.

> **Model Answer:**
> A **queuing system** is a stochastic service system in which customers arrive, wait in a queue if the server is busy, receive service, and then depart. The four principal characteristics are:
> 1. **Calling population** (finite/infinite)
> 2. **Arrival pattern** (e.g., Poisson)
> 3. **Queue discipline** (e.g., FIFO)
> 4. **Service mechanism** (number of servers and service time distribution)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Explain Kendall's notation. What does the model $(M/M/c):(FIFO/\infty)$ represent?

> **Model Answer:**
> **Kendall's notation** is a shorthand notation for classifying queuing systems, written as $(a/b/c):(d/e/f)$. The model $(M/M/c):(FIFO/\infty)$ represents a queuing system with:
> - **M:** Poisson (Markovian) arrival process
> - **M:** Exponential service times
> - **c:** $c$ parallel servers
> - **FIFO:** First-In-First-Out discipline
> - **$\infty$:** Infinite system capacity

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark ESE Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** Customers arrive at a railway reservation counter at a mean rate of 15 per hour. Service times are exponentially distributed with a mean of 3 minutes. Assuming steady-state conditions and an M/M/1 queuing model:

**(a) (7 Marks)** Find the traffic intensity, the probability that the system is empty, and the probability that there are exactly 3 customers in the system.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Compute the expected number of customers in the queue and in the system, the average waiting time in the queue, and the average time spent in the system.

#### Solution to Question A

**Given:** $\lambda = 15$/hr, mean service time $= 3$ min $= \dfrac{1}{20}$ hr, so $\mu = 20$/hr.

**(a) Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1: Traffic intensity**
$$\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu} = \dfrac{15}{20} = \dfrac{3}{4} = 0.75$$

**Step 2: Probability that the system is empty**
$$P_0 = 1 - \rho = 1 - 0.75 = 0.25$$

**Step 3: Probability that there are exactly 3 customers**
$$P_3 = (1 - \rho)\,\rho^{3} = 0.25 \times (0.75)^{3} = 0.25 \times 0.421875 = 0.1055$$

**(b) Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1: Expected number in queue**
$$L_q = \dfrac{\rho^{2}}{1 - \rho} = \dfrac{(0.75)^{2}}{0.25} = \dfrac{0.5625}{0.25} = 2.25 \text{ customers}$$

**Step 2: Expected number in system**
$$L = \dfrac{\rho}{1 - \rho} = \dfrac{0.75}{0.25} = 3.00 \text{ customers}$$

**Step 3: Expected waiting time in queue**
$$W_q = \dfrac{L_q}{\lambda} = \dfrac{2.25}{15} = 0.15 \text{ hr} = 9 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 4: Expected time in system**
$$W = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \dfrac{1}{20 - 15} = \dfrac{1}{5} \text{ hr} = 12 \text{ minutes}$$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]** A small post office has two clerks. Customers arrive at the rate of 20 per hour, and each clerk can serve at the rate of 15 customers per hour. Assume Poisson arrivals and exponential service times. (M/M/2 system)

**(a) (7 Marks)** Determine $P_0$, $P_1$, and $P_2$ (probability of zero, one, and two customers in the system).

**(b) (7 Marks)** Compute $L_q$, $L$, $W_q$, and $W$ for the system.

#### Solution to Question B

**Given:** $\lambda = 20$/hr, $\mu = 15$/hr, $c = 2$ servers.

**Step 1: Compute the offered load**
$$\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu} = \dfrac{20}{15} = \dfrac{4}{3}$$

**Step 2: Verify steady-state condition**
$$\dfrac{\rho}{c} = \dfrac{4/3}{2} = \dfrac{2}{3} < 1 \;\;\text{(Stable)}$$

**Step 3: Compute $P_0$**

$$P_0 = \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{c-1} \dfrac{\rho^{n}}{n!} + \dfrac{\rho^{c}}{c!\,(1 - \rho/c)} \right]^{-1}$$

$$P_0 = \left[ 1 + \rho + \dfrac{\rho^{2}}{2!\,(1 - \rho/2)} \right]^{-1} = \left[ 1 + \dfrac{4}{3} + \dfrac{(4/3)^{2}}{2 \times (1 - 2/3)} \right]^{-1}$$

$$P_0 = \left[ \dfrac{7}{3} + \dfrac{16/9}{2/3} \right]^{-1} = \left[ \dfrac{7}{3} + \dfrac{8}{3} \right]^{-1} = \left[ \dfrac{15}{3} \right]^{-1} = \dfrac{3}{15} = \dfrac{1}{5} = 0.2$$

**(a) Part (a) Solution:**

$$P_1 = P_0 \cdot \dfrac{\rho}{1!} = 0.2 \times \dfrac{4}{3} = \dfrac{0.8}{3} \approx 0.2667$$

$$P_2 = P_0 \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{2}}{2!} = 0.2 \times \dfrac{16/9}{2} = 0.2 \times \dfrac{8}{9} \approx 0.1778$$

**(b) Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1: $L_q$ for M/M/c**
$$L_q = \dfrac{P_0\,\rho^{c}\,\rho}{c!\,(c - \rho)^{2}} = \dfrac{(1/5)(4/3)^{2}(4/3)}{2! \times (2 - 4/3)^{2}}$$

$$L_q = \dfrac{(1/5)(16/9)(4/3)}{2 \times (2/3)^{2}} = \dfrac{64/135}{8/9} = \dfrac{64}{135} \times \dfrac{9}{8} = \dfrac{8}{15} \approx 0.5333$$

**Step 2: $L$ using Little's Law**
$$L = L_q + \rho = \dfrac{8}{15} + \dfrac{4}{3} = \dfrac{8}{15} + \dfrac{20}{15} = \dfrac{28}{15} \approx 1.8667$$

**Step 3: $W_q$ using Little's Law**
$$W_q = \dfrac{L_q}{\lambda} = \dfrac{8/15}{20} = \dfrac{8}{300} = \dfrac{2}{75} \text{ hr} = 1.6 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 4: $W$ using Little's Law**
$$W = W_q + \dfrac{1}{\mu} = \dfrac{2}{75} + \dfrac{1}{15} = \dfrac{2}{75} + \dfrac{5}{75} = \dfrac{7}{75} \text{ hr} \approx 5.6 \text{ minutes}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Convert service time to **hours** whenever $\lambda$ is in customers per hour. Mixing minutes and hours is the #1 cause of arithmetic errors.
> 2. **Forgetting steady-state check:** Always verify $\rho < 1$ (M/M/1) or $\rho/c < 1$ (M/M/c) **before** computing any performance measure. If violated, the system has no steady state.
> 3. **Misapplying Little's Law:** Remember $L = \lambda W$ and $L_q = \lambda W_q$. A frequent error is using $L = \lambda W_q$ or computing $L$ from $L + 1 = \lambda W$.
> 4. **Dropping the factorial in $P_0$:** The term $c!$ in the denominator of the M/M/c $P_0$ formula is mandatory — omitting it yields a wrong answer.
> 5. **Confusing $L$ and $L_q$:** $L_q$ excludes the customer in service. Therefore $L = L_q + \rho$ (in M/M/1, $\rho$ is the steady-state probability that the system is busy).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **queuing system** is fully described by the **six characteristics**: calling population, arrival pattern, queue discipline, service mechanism, system capacity, and number of phases.
- **Kendall's notation** $(a/b/c):(d/e/f)$ is the standard compact descriptor; **M = Markovian (Poisson/exponential)**, **D = Deterministic**, **G = General**, **$E_k$ = Erlang-k**.
- For **M/M/1** steady state to exist: **$\rho = \lambda/\mu < 1$**.
- The probability of an empty system is **$P_0 = 1 - \rho$**, and the steady-state distribution is **$P_n = (1 - \rho)\rho^{n}$**.
- The key performance metrics are: **$L$, $L_q$, $W$, $W_q$** — all linked by **Little's Law**: $L = \lambda W$ and $L_q = \lambda W_q$.
- For M/M/1: **$L = \rho/(1 - \rho)$**, **$L_q = \rho^{2}/(1 - \rho)$**, **$W = 1/(\mu - \lambda)$**, **$W_q = \rho/[\mu(1 - \rho)]$**.
- For M/M/c: stability requires **$\lambda < c\mu$** (i.e., $\rho < c$). The formulas for $L_q$ and $L$ involve the special $P_0$ summation.
- The **traffic intensity $\rho$** is also interpreted as the **long-run fraction of time the server is busy**.
- **FIFO** is the default discipline; problems must explicitly state otherwise to use LIFO, SIRO, or Priority.
- **Infinite capacity** is the default; finite-capacity systems $(M/M/1/K)$ have a different $P_0$ normalization over a finite sum from $n=0$ to $K$.
- **Transient-state** problems are **not** asked in KTU Module 4 — all analysis is **steady-state**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Measures in Queuing Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET528 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Performance Measures in Queuing Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Performance Measures in Queuing Systems** are quantitative metrics used to evaluate the operational efficiency, effectiveness, and service quality of a waiting line system. In the context of operations research, these measures describe the steady-state (long-run) behavior of a queueing system characterized by the standard Kendall's notation $X/Y/c/K/M/Z$ where performance is captured through probabilistic expectations of customer counts and waiting times.

> [!NOTE]
> **Kendall's Notation (Recap):** $X/Y/c/K/M/Z$ where $X$ = arrival process, $Y$ = service time distribution, $c$ = number of servers, $K$ = system capacity, $M$ = calling source size, $Z$ = queue discipline. The most studied KTU variant is the **M/M/1** and **M/M/c** (Markovian birth-death) systems.

### 1.2 The Two Fundamental Categories of Performance Measures

Performance measures in queuing theory bifurcate into two scientifically rigorous families:

| Category | Measure | Symbol | Dimension |
|----------|---------|--------|-----------|
| **Customer Count Measures** | Expected number of customers in the **system** | $L$ | customers |
| | Expected number of customers in the **queue** | $L_q$ | customers |
| **Time Measures** | Expected time a customer spends in the **system** | $W$ | time units |
| | Expected waiting time in the **queue** | $W_q$ | time units |
| **Server Utilization** | Probability that a server is busy | $\rho$ | dimensionless |
| **State Probabilities** | Probability of $n$ customers in system | $P_n$ | dimensionless |
| **Idle Probability** | Probability that the system is empty | $P_0$ | dimensionless |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Out-Patient Department (OPD)

Imagine a single doctor (server) in a small-town hospital OPD. Patients arrive randomly (Poisson arrival) and the doctor spends a random amount of time with each patient (exponential service).

- **$L$ (customers in system)**: If you walk in and count — patients already inside + the one being examined + you. This is the total **"load"** the OPD is carrying at any instant.
- **$L_q$ (customers in queue)**: Patients sitting on the bench waiting, *excluding* the one with the doctor. This is the **"waiting load"**.
- **$W$ (time in system)**: From the moment you enter the OPD till the moment you leave. This is **"total time"** the patient surrenders.
- **$W_q$ (waiting time)**: From entering the OPD till the doctor calls you in. The time spent **"staring at the wall"**.
- **$\rho$ (utilization)**: How busy the doctor is. If $\rho = 0.85$, the doctor is busy 85% of the time. If $\rho \to 1$, catastrophe is imminent.
- **$P_0$ (idle probability)**: Probability that the OPD is completely empty. Doctors love this number; patients dread it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight:** The course outcome (CO) for this module emphasizes *deriving* and *interpreting* these performance measures for **M/M/1**, **M/M/1/K**, **M/M/c**, and **M/M/c/K** queueing models. Numerical problems involving $\rho$, $L$, $L_q$, $W$, $W_q$ and **expected cost minimization** are the most frequently tested concepts.

### 1.4 The Underlying Assumptions (Birth-Death Process Foundation)

All performance measures rest on the **Birth-Death (BD) process** where:
- **Birth rate** $\lambda_n$ = arrival rate when $n$ customers are in the system
- **Death rate** $\mu_n$ = service (departure) rate when $n$ customers are in the system

For steady-state to exist: the average arrival rate $\lambda$ must be strictly less than the average service rate $\mu$ (for single-server systems), i.e., $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} < 1$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Behind Deriving Performance Measures — A 5-Step Blueprint

To systematically derive performance measures for any BD queue, the KTU examiner expects the following sequence:

**Step 1: Establish the Steady-State Balance Equations (Kolmogorov's Forward Equations)**

For a BD process in steady state, the rate of flow into state $n$ equals the rate of flow out of state $n$:

$$\lambda_{n-1} P_{n-1} + \mu_{n+1} P_{n+1} = (\lambda_n + \mu_n) P_n, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

**Step 2: Solve for State Probabilities $P_n$**

This gives the **geometric / truncated geometric** form depending on system capacity $K$.

**Step 3: Identify the Traffic Intensity / Utilization Factor $\rho$**

The dimensionless ratio $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$ quantifies system load.

**Step 4: Apply Little's Law (L = λW) for System ↔ Time Conversions**

**Step 5: Compute the expected values $L$, $L_q$, $W$, $W_q$** by summing the appropriate weighted probability distributions.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why balance equations?** Because in steady state, the system is statistically invariant — probability current into a state must equal probability current out.
- **Why geometric distribution?** Because birth-death queues are essentially *one-step* Markov chains — the ratio of consecutive probabilities is constant.
- **Why Little's Law?** It is *model-independent* and holds for virtually any queueing system (G/G/1, M/M/c, networks, etc.) — making it the most powerful general result in queueing theory.
- **Why is $\rho < 1$ crucial?** It is the necessary and sufficient condition for the **ergodic (steady-state) distribution to exist**. If $\rho \geq 1$, the queue explodes without bound.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Performance Measures Master Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Constants & Symbols (Use throughout your exam):**
> - $\lambda$ = mean arrival rate (customers/unit time)
> - $\mu$ = mean service rate per server (customers/unit time)
> - $c$ = number of identical parallel servers
> - $\rho$ = utilization factor (server-by-server, NOT total)
> - $P_0$ = probability the system is empty (idle)

| Model | $P_0$ | $L_q$ | $L$ | $W_q$ | $W$ | Utilization |
|-------|-------|-------|-----|-------|-----|-------------|
| **M/M/1** (Infinite capacity) | $1 - \rho$ | $\frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho}$ | $\frac{\rho}{1-\rho}$ | $\frac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda}$ | $\frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ | $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$ |
| **M/M/c** (Infinite capacity) | $\left[ \sum_{n=0}^{c-1} \frac{(c\rho)^n}{n!} + \frac{(c\rho)^c}{c! (1-\rho)} \right]^{-1}$ | $\frac{P_0 \cdot (c\rho)^c \cdot \rho}{c! (1-\rho)^2}$ | $L_q + \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | $\frac{L_q}{\lambda}$ | $W_q + \frac{1}{\mu}$ | $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{c\mu}$ |
| **M/M/1/K** (Finite capacity K) | $\frac{1 - \rho}{1 - \rho^{K+1}}$, $(\rho \neq 1)$ | $L - (1 - P_0)$ | $\frac{\rho}{1-\rho} - \frac{(K+1)\rho^{K+1}}{1-\rho^{K+1}}$ | $\frac{L_q}{\lambda_{eff}}$ | $W_q + \frac{1}{\mu}$ | $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$ |
| **M/M/c/K** (Finite capacity, c servers) | See expansion form | Complex — computed via $P_n$ table | $L_q + c\rho(1 - P_K)$ | $\frac{L_q}{\lambda_{eff}}$ | $W_q + \frac{1}{\mu}$ | $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{c\mu}$ |
| **M/M/1/K/K** (Finite population) | Requires effective $\lambda$ | Use birth-death table with $\lambda_n = (K-n)\lambda$ | $\sum n P_n$ | $\frac{L_q}{\lambda_{eff}}$ | $W_q + \frac{1}{\mu}$ | – |

> [!NOTE]
> **Effective Arrival Rate $\lambda_{eff}$**: For finite capacity systems, customers may be *blocked/rejected*. Thus, $\lambda_{eff} = \lambda(1 - P_K)$ where $P_K$ is the probability the system is full.

### 2.4 Engineering & Real-World Utility

These performance measures are the **backbone of capacity planning** in:
- **Telecommunications**: Erlang's M/M/c formulas (Erlang-B, Erlang-C) dimension cellular base stations and call centers.
- **Manufacturing**: Optimal number of machines on a production line (M/M/c) minimizing cost of waiting + service.
- **Healthcare**: Staffing nurses in emergency rooms.
- **Logistics**: Determining number of loading docks at a warehouse.
- **Computer Systems**: Modeling CPU scheduling (M/M/1 with FCFS discipline).

> [!TIP]
> **The Five Magic Equations (KTU Favourite):**
> 1. $L = L_q + \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$ (Customers in system = in queue + being served, on average)
> 2. $W = W_q + \frac{1}{\mu}$ (Time in system = time in queue + service time, on average)
> 3. $L = \lambda W$ (Little's Law)
> 4. $L_q = \lambda W_q$ (Little's Law applied to the queue)
> 5. $P(\text{wait} > t) = e^{-(c\mu - \lambda)t}$ (exponential waiting time in M/M/c)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — M/M/1 Performance Measures

**Starting assumption:** Poisson arrivals with rate $\lambda$, exponential service with rate $\mu$, single server, infinite capacity, FCFS discipline.

**Step 1: Write the steady-state balance equations**

For $n = 0$:
$$\mu_1 P_1 = \lambda_0 P_0 \implies \mu P_1 = \lambda P_0$$

For $n \geq 1$:
$$\lambda_{n-1} P_{n-1} + \mu_{n+1} P_{n+1} = (\lambda_n + \mu_n) P_n$$
$$\lambda P_{n-1} + \mu P_{n+1} = (\lambda + \mu) P_n$$

**Step 2: Solve recursively**

From the $n=0$ equation:
$$P_1 = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} P_0 = \rho P_0$$

From the $n=1$ equation:
$$\lambda P_0 + \mu P_2 = (\lambda + \mu) P_1$$
$$\mu P_2 = (\lambda + \mu) \rho P_0 - \lambda P_0 = \lambda \rho P_0$$
$$P_2 = \rho^2 P_0$$

By induction, $P_n = \rho^n P_0$.

**Step 3: Apply the normalization condition** $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P_n = 1$

$$P_0 \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \rho^n = P_0 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \rho} = 1$$

$$P_0 = 1 - \rho$$

**Step 4: Derive $L$ (expected number in system)**

$$L = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n P_n = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \rho^n (1 - \rho) = (1 - \rho) \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n \rho^n$$

Using the known series identity $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n x^n = \frac{x}{(1-x)^2}$ with $x = \rho$:

$$L = (1 - \rho) \cdot \frac{\rho}{(1 - \rho)^2} = \frac{\rho}{1 - \rho}$$

**Step 5: Derive $L_q$ (expected number in queue)**

In M/M/1, the system has at most 1 customer being served. Thus, the customer in service contributes $\frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \rho$ to $L$. By the marginal identity:

$$L_q = L - \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} - \rho = \frac{\rho - \rho(1-\rho)}{1-\rho} = \frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho}$$

**Step 6: Apply Little's Law to obtain time measures**

$$W = \frac{L}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} = \frac{\rho}{\lambda(1-\rho)} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$$

$$W_q = \frac{L_q}{\lambda} = \frac{\rho^2}{\lambda(1-\rho)} = \frac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda}$$

**Step 7: The W-Wq identity** (verifies consistency)

$$W = W_q + \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda} + \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{\rho \mu + \mu - \lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)} = \frac{\mu - \lambda + \lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} \;\checkmark$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — M/M/c Performance Measures

For $c$ parallel identical servers, each with service rate $\mu$, total service capacity is $c\mu$. The per-server utilization is $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{c\mu}$.

**Step 1: Define the birth and death rates**

$$\lambda_n = \lambda \quad \text{for all } n$$
$$\mu_n = \begin{cases} n\mu & n = 1, 2, \ldots, c \\ c\mu & n \geq c \end{cases}$$

**Step 2: Solve the balance equations**

For $1 \leq n \leq c$:
$$P_n = \frac{\lambda^n}{n! \mu^n} P_0 = \frac{(c\rho)^n}{n!} P_0$$

For $n > c$:
$$P_n = \frac{\lambda^n}{c! \mu^c (c\mu)^{n-c}} P_0 = \frac{(c\rho)^n}{c! \, c^{n-c}} P_0 = \frac{(c\rho)^c}{c!} \cdot \rho^{n-c} P_0$$

**Step 3: Normalize to find $P_0$**

$$P_0 = \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{c-1} \frac{(c\rho)^n}{n!} + \frac{(c\rho)^c}{c!} \sum_{n=c}^{\infty} \rho^{n-c} \right]^{-1}$$

The geometric series sums to $\frac{1}{1-\rho}$:

$$P_0 = \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{c-1} \frac{(c\rho)^n}{n!} + \frac{(c\rho)^c}{c! (1-\rho)} \right]^{-1}$$

**Step 4: Compute $L_q$ — Customers Waiting (not being served)**

$$L_q = \sum_{n=c}^{\infty} (n - c) P_n = \frac{(c\rho)^c \rho}{c! (1-\rho)^2} P_0$$

This is the most frequently tested M/M/c formula in KTU exams.

**Step 5: Compute $L$, $W$, $W_q$**

$$L = L_q + \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = L_q + c\rho$$

$$W_q = \frac{L_q}{\lambda}, \qquad W = W_q + \frac{1}{\mu}$$

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A small bank has a single ATM. Customers arrive at rate $\lambda = 8$ per hour. The ATM serves customers at $\mu = 10$ per hour. Compute all performance measures for the M/M/1 model.

**Solution:**

$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8$$

$$P_0 = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2$$

$$L = \frac{0.8}{1 - 0.8} = \frac{0.8}{0.2} = 4 \text{ customers}$$

$$L_q = \frac{(0.8)^2}{1 - 0.8} = \frac{0.64}{0.2} = 3.2 \text{ customers}$$

$$W = \frac{1}{10 - 8} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \text{ hours} = 30 \text{ minutes}$$

$$W_q = \frac{0.8}{10 - 8} = 0.4 \text{ hours} = 24 \text{ minutes}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for Exam:** The ATM is busy 80% of the time. On average, 4 customers are in the system (3.2 waiting + 0.8 being served). Each customer spends 30 minutes total, 24 minutes of which is waiting. This signals a need for adding a second ATM.

### 3.4 Expected Cost Optimization (KTU High-Yield)

For an M/M/1 system with cost structure:
- $C_s$ = cost per server per unit time
- $C_w$ = cost per customer waiting per unit time (per unit time, per customer)

**Total Expected Cost per Unit Time:**
$$\text{TC}(c) = c \cdot C_s + L \cdot C_w$$

The optimal number of servers $c^*$ is the smallest integer $c$ such that:
$$\text{TC}(c^* - 1) \geq \text{TC}(c^*) \quad \text{and} \quad \text{TC}(c^* + 1) \geq \text{TC}(c^*)$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

flowchart TD

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Define the term "performance measures" in queuing systems. List any four key performance measures used to evaluate an M/M/1 queue.**

**Model Answer:**

Performance measures are the quantitative steady-state metrics that characterize the operational effectiveness of a queuing system. The four key measures for an M/M/1 queue are:

1. **$L$** — Average number of customers in the system (including the one being served).
2. **$L_q$** — Average number of customers waiting in the queue.
3. **$W$** — Average time a customer spends in the system.
4. **$W_q$** — Average time a customer spends waiting in the queue before service begins.

These are linked by Little's Law: $L = \lambda W$ and $L_q = \lambda W_q$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] State Little's Law and explain its significance in queuing analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

**Little's Law:** The long-run average number of customers in a stable queueing system $L$ is equal to the long-run average arrival rate $\lambda$ multiplied by the long-run average time a customer spends in the system $W$.

$$L = \lambda W$$

**Significance:**
- It is *distribution-free* — applies to G/G/s, M/M/c, M/G/1, etc.
- It links **customer-count** measures (cross-sectional) to **time** measures (longitudinal).
- It is the fundamental tool to convert between system-level metrics and per-customer metrics without re-derivation.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing $\rho$ for total utilization vs. per-server utilization. In M/M/c, $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{c\mu}$ — *not* $\frac{\lambda}{\mu}$. Each server's individual utilization is $\rho$, total system utilization is $c\rho$.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to verify $\rho < 1$ before quoting formulas. If $\rho \geq 1$, **no steady state** exists and formulas are invalid.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Mixing up $W$ and $W_q$ — the difference is exactly $\frac{1}{\mu}$ (mean service time). Marks are deducted if $W = W_q$ is reported.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In M/M/c problems, students forget to evaluate the infinite sum $\sum_{n=c}^{\infty} \rho^{n-c} = \frac{1}{1-\rho}$ in the $P_0$ denominator.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** In cost-optimization, failing to identify the *smallest* integer $c$ that minimizes total cost.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

(a) **Derive the steady-state probability distribution $P_n$ and the performance measures $L$, $L_q$, $W$, $W_q$ for an M/M/1 queueing system.** (7 Marks)

(b) **A car wash facility operates with a single bay. Cars arrive at a rate of 4 per hour (Poisson) and service time averages 10 minutes (exponential). Compute all performance measures. Comment on whether adding a second bay would be justified.** (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks)**

(Already shown exhaustively in Section 3.1 above. Key results to present:)

- Balance equations: $\mu P_1 = \lambda P_0$ and $\lambda P_{n-1} + \mu P_{n+1} = (\lambda + \mu) P_n$.
- General solution: $P_n = \rho^n (1 - \rho)$ where $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$, $0 < \rho < 1$.
- Performance measures:

$$L = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho}, \quad L_q = \frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho}, \quad W = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}, \quad W_q = \frac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda}$$

**Stability:** $\rho < 1$ ensures steady-state convergence.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

Given: $\lambda = 4$ cars/hour, mean service time $= 10$ min $= \frac{1}{6}$ hour, so $\mu = 6$ cars/hour.

$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{4}{6} = 0.6667$$

$$P_0 = 1 - 0.6667 = 0.3333$$

$$L = \frac{0.6667}{1 - 0.6667} = \frac{0.6667}{0.3333} = 2 \text{ cars}$$

$$L_q = \frac{(0.6667)^2}{0.3333} = \frac{0.4444}{0.3333} = 1.333 \text{ cars}$$

$$W = \frac{1}{6 - 4} = 0.5 \text{ hours} = 30 \text{ minutes}$$

$$W_q = \frac{0.6667}{6 - 4} = 0.3333 \text{ hours} = 20 \text{ minutes}$$

**Justification for Second Bay:** With $\rho = 0.667$, the single bay is busy 66.7% of the time and the average waiting time is 20 minutes. If customer satisfaction threshold is 10 minutes, the second bay is justified. If customers tolerate 20 minutes, single bay is sufficient.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

(a) **Derive the expressions for $P_0$, $L_q$, $L$, $W_q$, and $W$ for an M/M/c queueing model with $c$ identical servers.** (7 Marks)

(b) **A call center has 3 agents. Calls arrive at rate $\lambda = 12$ per hour (Poisson), and each agent handles calls at rate $\mu = 5$ per hour (exponential). Compute $P_0$, $L_q$, $L$, $W_q$, and $W$.** (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks)**

(Reference Section 3.2 of these notes. Key formulas to present:)

$$\lambda_n = \lambda, \quad \mu_n = \begin{cases} n\mu, & n \leq c \\ c\mu, & n > c \end{cases}$$

$$P_n = \begin{cases} \dfrac{(c\rho)^n}{n!} P_0, & n \leq c \\[2mm] \dfrac{(c\rho)^c \rho^{n-c}}{c!} P_0, & n > c \end{cases}$$

$$P_0 = \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{c-1} \frac{(c\rho)^n}{n!} + \frac{(c\rho)^c}{c!(1-\rho)} \right]^{-1}$$

$$L_q = \frac{P_0 (c\rho)^c \rho}{c! (1-\rho)^2}$$

$$L = L_q + c\rho = L_q + \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$$

$$W_q = \frac{L_q}{\lambda}, \quad W = W_q + \frac{1}{\mu}$$

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

Given: $\lambda = 12$ per hour, $\mu = 5$ per hour, $c = 3$.

**Compute $\rho$** (per-server utilization):
$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{c\mu} = \frac{12}{3 \times 5} = \frac{12}{15} = 0.8$$

Since $\rho = 0.8 < 1$, steady state exists. ✓

**Compute $P_0$:**
$$c\rho = 3 \times 0.8 = 2.4$$

$$P_0 = \left[ \frac{(2.4)^0}{0!} + \frac{(2.4)^1}{1!} + \frac{(2.4)^2}{2!} + \frac{(2.4)^3}{3!(1-0.8)} \right]^{-1}$$

$$P_0 = \left[ 1 + 2.4 + \frac{5.76}{2} + \frac{13.824}{6 \times 0.2} \right]^{-1}$$

$$P_0 = \left[ 1 + 2.4 + 2.88 + \frac{13.824}{1.2} \right]^{-1}$$

$$P_0 = \left[ 1 + 2.4 + 2.88 + 11.52 \right]^{-1} = \frac{1}{17.8} = 0.0562$$

**Compute $L_q$:**
$$L_q = \frac{P_0 (c\rho)^c \rho}{c! (1-\rho)^2} = \frac{0.0562 \times 11.52 \times 0.8}{6 \times (0.2)^2} = \frac{0.5180}{0.24} = 2.158 \text{ callers}$$

**Compute $L$:**
$$L = L_q + c\rho = 2.158 + 2.4 = 4.558 \text{ callers}$$

**Compute $W_q$:**
$$W_q = \frac{L_q}{\lambda} = \frac{2.158}{12} = 0.1798 \text{ hours} = 10.79 \text{ minutes}$$

**Compute $W$:**
$$W = W_q + \frac{1}{\mu} = 0.1798 + 0.2 = 0.3798 \text{ hours} = 22.79 \text{ minutes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** With 3 agents, callers wait an average of ~10.8 minutes before being served, and the total call duration is ~22.8 minutes. The system is operating at 80% per-server utilization, which is near the danger threshold — adding a 4th agent should be considered if waiting time reduction is critical.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **🔥 High-Frequency KTU Exam Triggers — Memorize These:**

- ✅ **Kendall's Notation M/M/1** = Poisson arrivals, Exponential service, **1 server**, infinite capacity.
- ✅ **Kendall's Notation M/M/c** = Poisson arrivals, Exponential service, **c servers**, infinite capacity.
- ✅ **Kendall's Notation M/M/1/K** = Single server, **system capacity K** (queue + service ≤ K).
- ✅ **Stability Condition (M/M/1)**: $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} < 1$. If $\rho \geq 1$, **NO** steady state.
- ✅ **Stability Condition (M/M/c)**: $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{c\mu} < 1$. Note the **$c$** in denominator!
- ✅ **Little's Law Variants**:
  - $L = \lambda W$
  - $L_q = \lambda W_q$
  - $L - L_q = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$ (avg customers being served)
  - $W - W_q = \frac{1}{\mu}$ (avg service time)
- ✅ **M/M/1 Formulas** (most tested):
  - $P_0 = 1 - \rho$
  - $L = \frac{\rho}{1 - \rho}$
  - $L_q = \frac{\rho^2}{1 - \rho}$
  - $W = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$
  - $W_q = \frac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda}$
- ✅ **M/M/c Formulas** (Erlang-C system):
  - $L_q = \frac{P_0 (c\rho)^c \rho}{c! (1 - \rho)^2}$ — **Golden Formula for KTU**
  - $P_0$ involves summation of $(c\rho)^n / n!$ from $n=0$ to $c-1$ plus the geometric term.
- ✅ **M/M/1/K (Finite Capacity)**: Use $P_n = \frac{(1-\rho)\rho^n}{1-\rho^{K+1}}$ for $\rho \neq 1$, and $P_n = \frac{1}{K+1}$ for $\rho = 1$.
- ✅ **Effective Arrival Rate for Finite Systems**: $\lambda_{eff} = \lambda(1 - P_K)$.
- ✅ **Cost Optimization**: Minimize $\text{TC}(c) = cC_s + LC_w$ over integer values of $c$.
- ✅ **Limit Behavior**: As $\rho \to 1^-$, $L \to \infty$ — system explodes; **never operate near $\rho = 1$**.
- ✅ **Service Discipline**: FCFS is the default. Performance measures (L, Lq, W, Wq) are **independent** of discipline; only individual customer metrics change.
- ✅ **Key Numerical Tip**: $W \mu = \frac{1}{1-\rho}$ — handy for quick cross-checks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Mantra for KTU Exam Day:** *"Births equal deaths in steady state. Solve the BD equations. Normalize. Use Little's Law. Verify $\rho < 1$. Report. Move on."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classic Queuing Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET528 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classic Queuing Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Classic Queuing Model?

A **Classic Queuing Model** is a mathematically rigorous, parameterized representation of a waiting-line (queue) system in which the arrival pattern of customers and the service mechanism of servers are governed by well-defined stochastic processes — most commonly, the **Poisson process** for arrivals and the **Exponential distribution** for service times. These models form the analytical backbone of **Birth-Death Process based queueing theory**, allowing engineers to compute steady-state performance measures such as average queue length, average waiting time, server utilization, and the probability of customer blocking.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the study of queuing models falls under **Birth-Death Processes**, where the "birth" of a customer increments the system state by one, and the "death" (service completion) decrements it. The most celebrated single-line of models — collectively called the **M/M/c family** — assumes **Markovian (memoryless)** inter-arrival and service times, which leads to tractable steady-state difference equations solvable in closed form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kendall-Lee Notation (a / b / c : d / e / f)** is the universal shorthand used to classify any queue:
> - **a** = Arrival process (e.g., **M** for Markovian/Poisson, **D** for Deterministic, **G** for General)
> - **b** = Service time distribution (same codes as **a**)
> - **c** = Number of parallel servers
> - **d** = Maximum system capacity (including those in service)
> - **e** = Population size (calling source)
> - **f** = Service discipline (e.g., **FCFS**, **LCFS**, **SIRO**)
>
> *If d, e, f are omitted, the defaults are* **∞ / ∞ / FCFS**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Coffee Shop Counter** ☕
> Imagine a single-barista coffee shop. Customers walk in at a **random rate λ** (arrivals) and the barista serves them at an average rate **μ**. If the barista is busy, customers form a single line. This is a classic **M/M/1** queue. Now extend this:
> - If the shop has **c** identical baristas, it becomes an **M/M/c** queue.
> - If only **K** customers can wait inside (no more space after that), it is an **M/M/1/K** queue.
> - If there are no servers at all but infinitely many clerks helping simultaneously, it is an **M/M/∞** queue.
>
> The **manager's job** is to balance the trade-off: hiring more servers (lower waiting) costs more money (higher utilization loss). Queuing models tell the manager **exactly** how many servers are needed to keep waiting time below a target.

## 1.3 Why Study These Models?

| Application Domain | Example Scenario | Queue Type |
|---|---|---|
| Telecommunications | Call centres, packet routers | M/M/c, M/M/c/K |
| Manufacturing | Machine shops, assembly lines | M/M/1, M/M/c |
| Healthcare | Hospital OPDs, emergency rooms | M/M/c with priorities |
| Computer Systems | CPU scheduling, disk I/O | M/M/1, M/M/c |
| Transportation | Toll booths, traffic signals | M/M/c, finite population |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Performance Metrics** (computed by every model in this module):
> - **L<sub>s</sub>** = Expected number of customers **in the system** (queue + service)
> - **L<sub>q</sub>** = Expected number of customers **in the queue** only
> - **W<sub>s</sub>** = Expected time a customer spends **in the system**
> - **W<sub>q</sub>** = Expected time a customer spends **in the queue** only
> - **ρ** = **Server utilization factor** (traffic intensity)
> - **P<sub>n</sub>** = Steady-state probability of exactly **n** customers in the system
> - **P<sub>0</sub>** = Probability that the system is **empty**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Birth-Death Process Foundation

Every classic queuing model in this module is a special case of a **Birth-Death (B-D) Process**, which is a continuous-time Markov chain on the state space {0, 1, 2, 3, ...} where:
- **λ<sub>n</sub>** = transition rate from state **n** to state **n+1** (arrival rate when **n** are present)
- **μ<sub>n</sub>** = transition rate from state **n** to state **n-1** (service rate when **n** are present)
- For states **n ≥ 0** and **n ≥ 1** respectively.

### 2.1.1 The Steady-State Balance Equations

By equating the **flow into** and **flow out of** each state in equilibrium, we obtain the recursion:

$$P_{n+1} = \frac{\lambda_{n}}{\mu_{n+1}} \, P_{n} \quad \text{for } n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

Expanding telescopically:

$$P_{n} = P_{0} \prod_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{\lambda_{i}}{\mu_{i+1}}$$

The normalizing condition is:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P_{n} = 1$$

This single formulation **unifies** all the classic models — the difference is merely the choice of λ<sub>n</sub> and μ<sub>n</sub>.

## 2.2 Model-by-Model Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Notation:** λ = mean arrival rate, μ = mean service rate per server, c = number of servers, K = maximum system capacity, ρ = λ/(cμ) = **traffic intensity (utilization)**.

### 2.2.1 Model M/M/1 (Single Server, Infinite Capacity)

This is the simplest and most frequently tested model. It is **stable** only if **ρ = λ/μ < 1**.

| Quantity | Formula | Condition |
|---|---|---|
| **Steady-state probability** | $P_{n} = (1 - \rho) \, \rho^{n}$ | $\rho < 1$ |
| **Empty-system probability** | $P_{0} = 1 - \rho$ | $\rho < 1$ |
| **Expected number in system** | $L_{s} = \dfrac{\rho}{1 - \rho} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu - \lambda}$ | $\rho < 1$ |
| **Expected number in queue** | $L_{q} = \dfrac{\rho^{2}}{1 - \rho} = \dfrac{\lambda^{2}}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)}$ | $\rho < 1$ |
| **Expected time in system** | $W_{s} = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ | Little's Law |
| **Expected waiting time in queue** | $W_{q} = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)}$ | Little's Law |

### 2.2.2 Model M/M/c (c Parallel Servers, Infinite Capacity)

The probability structure has a piecewise form because the service rate jumps from **nμ** (when n < c servers busy) to **cμ** (when all c are busy).

| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| **Traffic intensity** | $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{c \mu} < 1$ |
| **Empty-system probability** | $P_{0} = \left[ \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{c-1} \frac{(c\rho)^{n}}{n!} + \frac{(c\rho)^{c}}{c!} \cdot \frac{1}{1-\rho} \right]^{-1}$ |
| **State probability (n < c)** | $P_{n} = \dfrac{(c\rho)^{n}}{n!} \, P_{0}$ |
| **State probability (n ≥ c)** | $P_{n} = \dfrac{c^{c} \, \rho^{n}}{c!} \, P_{0}$ |
| **Expected number in queue** | $L_{q} = P_{0} \cdot \dfrac{(c\rho)^{c} \, \rho}{c! \, (1-\rho)^{2}}$ |
| **Expected waiting in queue** | $W_{q} = \dfrac{L_{q}}{\lambda}$ |
| **Expected time in system** | $W_{s} = W_{q} + \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ |
| **Expected number in system** | $L_{s} = L_{q} + \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ |
| **Probability of waiting** | $P(\text{wait}) = \dfrac{(c\rho)^{c}}{c! \, (1-\rho)} \, P_{0}$ |

### 2.2.3 Model M/M/∞ (Infinite Servers, Infinite Capacity)

Every arriving customer gets an instant idle server. The system **never** reaches saturation.

| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| **Empty-system probability** | $P_{0} = e^{-\lambda/\mu}$ |
| **State probability (Poisson)** | $P_{n} = \dfrac{(\lambda/\mu)^{n} \, e^{-\lambda/\mu}}{n!}$ |
| **Expected number in system** | $L_{s} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ |
| **Expected number in queue** | $L_{q} = 0$ (no waiting) |
| **Expected time in system** | $W_{s} = \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ |
| **Expected waiting time** | $W_{q} = 0$ |

### 2.2.4 Model M/M/1/K (Single Server, Finite Capacity K)

Used when the system can hold at most **K** customers (including the one in service). If a new arrival finds the system full, it is **lost** (blocked).

| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| **Traffic intensity** | $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ (can be ≥ 1) |
| **Steady-state probability** | $P_{n} = \dfrac{(1-\rho) \, \rho^{n}}{1 - \rho^{K+1}}$ for $\rho \neq 1$ |
| **Empty-system probability** | $P_{0} = \dfrac{1 - \rho}{1 - \rho^{K+1}}$ (when $\rho \neq 1$) |
| **Empty-system probability** | $P_{0} = \dfrac{1}{K+1}$ (when $\rho = 1$) |
| **Expected number in system** | $L_{s} = \dfrac{\rho}{1-\rho} - \dfrac{(K+1)\rho^{K+1}}{1-\rho^{K+1}}$ |
| **Expected number in queue** | $L_{q} = L_{s} - (1 - P_{0})$ |
| **Effective arrival rate** | $\lambda_{e} = \lambda (1 - P_{K})$ |
| **Expected time in system** | $W_{s} = \dfrac{L_{s}}{\lambda_{e}}$ |
| **Expected waiting in queue** | $W_{q} = W_{s} - \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ |

### 2.2.5 Model M/M/c/K (c Servers, Finite Capacity K)

The most general "examinable" model combining finite servers and finite capacity.

| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| **Traffic intensity per server** | $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{c \mu}$ (can exceed 1) |
| **Empty-system probability** | $P_{0} = \left[ \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{c} \frac{(c\rho)^{n}}{n!} + \dfrac{c^{c}}{c!} \cdot \rho^{c} \cdot \dfrac{1 - \rho^{K-c}}{1 - \rho} \right]^{-1}$ (for $\rho \neq 1$) |
| **State probability (n ≤ c)** | $P_{n} = \dfrac{(c\rho)^{n}}{n!} \, P_{0}$ |
| **State probability (c < n ≤ K)** | $P_{n} = \dfrac{c^{c} \, \rho^{n}}{c!} \, P_{0}$ |
| **Blocking probability** | $P_{K} = \dfrac{c^{c} \, \rho^{K}}{c!} \, P_{0}$ |
| **Effective arrival rate** | $\lambda_{e} = \lambda (1 - P_{K})$ |
| **Expected number in queue** | $L_{q} = P_{0} \cdot \dfrac{c^{c} \, \rho^{c+1}}{c! \, (1-\rho)^{2}} \cdot \left[ 1 - (1-\rho)^{K-c} - (K-c)\rho^{K-c}(1-\rho) \right]$ |
| **Expected number in system** | $L_{s} = L_{q} + c\rho(1 - P_{K})$ |
| **Expected time in system** | $W_{s} = \dfrac{L_{s}}{\lambda_{e}}$ |
| **Expected waiting in queue** | $W_{q} = \dfrac{L_{q}}{\lambda_{e}}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Little's Law** is the universal unifying identity: **L = λW**. Whenever you know two of {L, λ, W}, the third is determined. It applies to *any* queueing system in steady state (not just M/M/c).

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

- **Capacity Planning**: Decide the **minimum number of servers** *c* to keep **W<sub>q</sub>** below a service-level agreement (SLA) threshold.
- **Cost Minimization**: Total Cost = (Server cost × c) + (Waiting cost × L<sub>q</sub>). Differentiate or tabulate to find the optimal *c*.
- **System Design**: In telecommunication networks, the **Erlang-B formula** (derived from M/M/c/K) gives the blocking probability of a trunk group with **c** lines and Poisson traffic.
- **Industrial Engineering**: Models machine breakdown-repair cycles (where μ represents the repair rate) as finite-capacity queues.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the M/M/1 Steady-State Distribution

**Setup.** Consider a single-server queue with:
- Poisson arrivals at rate **λ** (so λ<sub>n</sub> = λ for all n)
- Exponential service times at rate **μ** (so μ<sub>n</sub> = μ for all n ≥ 1)
- Infinite capacity, FCFS discipline.

### Step 1 — Write the Balance Equations

Equate **rate in = rate out** at each state *n*:

**At state 0:** $\quad \mu \, P_{1} = \lambda \, P_{0}$

**At state n ≥ 1:** $\quad \lambda \, P_{n-1} + \mu \, P_{n+1} = (\lambda + \mu) \, P_{n}$

### Step 2 — Solve the Recursion

From the state-0 equation:

$$P_{1} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} \, P_{0} = \rho \, P_{0}$$

For *n = 1*, the balance equation becomes:

$$\mu \, P_{2} = \lambda \, P_{1} + (\lambda + \mu) P_{1} - \lambda \, P_{0} = \lambda \, P_{1} - \lambda \, P_{0} + \mu \, P_{1} - \mu \, P_{1} \text{ ... (simplify)}$$

Working through the standard B-D recursion directly:

$$P_{n+1} = \frac{\lambda_{n}}{\mu_{n+1}} \, P_{n} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} \, P_{n} = \rho \, P_{n}$$

By induction:

$$P_{n} = \rho^{n} \, P_{0} \quad \text{for all } n \geq 0$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Normalizing Condition

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P_{n} = P_{0} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \rho^{n} = P_{0} \cdot \frac{1}{1-\rho} = 1$$

This geometric series converges only if **|ρ| < 1**, i.e., **ρ < 1** (since both rates are positive). Therefore:

$$P_{0} = 1 - \rho$$

And the complete probability law is:

$$\boxed{P_{n} = (1 - \rho) \, \rho^{n}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Performance Measures

**Expected number in system (L<sub>s</sub>):**

$$L_{s} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \, P_{n} = (1-\rho) \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n \rho^{n} = (1-\rho) \cdot \frac{\rho}{(1-\rho)^{2}} = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho}$$

Using the known identity $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n x^{n} = \frac{x}{(1-x)^{2}}$ for $|x| < 1$.

**Expected number in queue (L<sub>q</sub>):**
Note that the expected number being served equals $1 \cdot P(\text{server busy}) = 1 \cdot (1 - P_{0}) = 1 - (1-\rho) = \rho$. Hence:

$$L_{q} = L_{s} - \rho = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} - \rho = \frac{\rho - \rho(1-\rho)}{1-\rho} = \frac{\rho^{2}}{1-\rho}$$

**Time in queue (W<sub>q</sub>) and time in system (W<sub>s</sub>) by Little's Law:**

$$W_{q} = \frac{L_{q}}{\lambda} = \frac{\rho^{2}}{(1-\rho)\lambda} = \frac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda}$$

$$W_{s} = W_{q} + \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda} + \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{\rho \mu + \mu - \lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why W<sub>s</sub> = 1/(μ − λ)?** This is one of the most beautiful results in queueing theory: a customer's expected sojourn time equals the expected service time of one customer, **de-rated** by the difference between the service rate and arrival rate. As ρ → 1, W<sub>s</sub> → ∞ (the system becomes unstable).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — M/M/1 Model

> **[Worked Example 3.2.1]** A single-barber salon has a Poisson arrival rate of **λ = 8 customers/hour**. The service time is exponentially distributed with mean **6 minutes** (i.e., μ = 10 customers/hour). Find (i) P<sub>0</sub>, (ii) L<sub>s</sub>, (iii) L<sub>q</sub>, (iv) W<sub>s</sub>, (v) W<sub>q</sub>, (vi) the probability that more than 4 customers are in the system.

**Solution.**

**Step 1: Compute ρ.**

$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8 < 1 \quad \text{(system is stable ✓)}$$

**Step 2: P<sub>0</sub>.**

$$P_{0} = 1 - \rho = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2$$

**Step 3: L<sub>s</sub>.**

$$L_{s} = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} = \frac{0.8}{0.2} = 4 \text{ customers}$$

**Step 4: L<sub>q</sub>.**

$$L_{q} = \frac{\rho^{2}}{1-\rho} = \frac{0.64}{0.2} = 3.2 \text{ customers}$$

**Step 5: W<sub>s</sub>.**

$$W_{s} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{1}{10 - 8} = 0.5 \text{ hours} = 30 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 6: W<sub>q</sub>.**

$$W_{q} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)} = \frac{8}{10 \times 2} = 0.4 \text{ hours} = 24 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 7: P(N > 4).**

$$P(N > 4) = \sum_{n=5}^{\infty} \rho^{n}(1-\rho) = \rho^{5} = (0.8)^{5} = 0.32768 \approx 32.77\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** In M/M/1, $P(N > n) = \rho^{n+1}$ — a popular short-form that frequently appears as a sub-part. **Don't confuse it** with $P(N \geq n) = \rho^{n}$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — M/M/c Model

> **[Worked Example 3.3.1]** A bank's drive-through window has **c = 2** servers. Customers arrive at rate **λ = 30/hour** (Poisson) and each server serves at rate **μ = 20/hour** (Exponential). Compute P<sub>0</sub>, L<sub>q</sub>, W<sub>q</sub>, and W<sub>s</sub>.

**Solution.**

**Step 1: Compute ρ.**

$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{c\mu} = \frac{30}{2 \times 20} = 0.75 < 1 \quad \text{(stable ✓)}$$

**Step 2: P<sub>0</sub>.**

$$P_{0} = \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{1} \frac{(c\rho)^{n}}{n!} + \frac{(c\rho)^{c}}{c!} \cdot \frac{1}{1-\rho} \right]^{-1}$$

$$= \left[ \frac{(1.5)^{0}}{0!} + \frac{(1.5)^{1}}{1!} + \frac{(1.5)^{2}}{2!} \cdot \frac{1}{0.25} \right]^{-1}$$

$$= \left[ 1 + 1.5 + \frac{2.25}{2} \times 4 \right]^{-1} = \left[ 1 + 1.5 + 4.5 \right]^{-1} = \frac{1}{7} \approx 0.1429$$

**Step 3: L<sub>q</sub>.**

$$L_{q} = \frac{P_{0} \, (c\rho)^{c} \, \rho}{c! \, (1-\rho)^{2}} = \frac{(1/7) \times 2.25 \times 0.75}{2 \times 0.0625} = \frac{0.2411}{0.125} \approx 1.929 \text{ customers}$$

**Step 4: W<sub>q</sub>.**

$$W_{q} = \frac{L_{q}}{\lambda} = \frac{1.929}{30} \approx 0.0643 \text{ hours} \approx 3.86 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 5: W<sub>s</sub>.**

$$W_{s} = W_{q} + \frac{1}{\mu} = 0.0643 + 0.05 = 0.1143 \text{ hours} \approx 6.86 \text{ minutes}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — M/M/1/K (Finite Capacity) Model

> **[Worked Example 3.4.1]** A small parking lot has a single attendant and space for **K = 4** cars (including the one being served at the gate). Cars arrive at **λ = 10/hour** (Poisson) and the service (issuing a ticket) takes an average of **5 minutes** (μ = 12/hour). Find P<sub>0</sub>, P<sub>4</sub> (lot full), L<sub>s</sub>, L<sub>q</sub>, and the effective arrival rate.

**Solution.**

**Step 1: Compute ρ.**

$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{10}{12} = \frac{5}{6} \approx 0.8333$$

**Step 2: P<sub>0</sub>.**

$$P_{0} = \frac{1-\rho}{1-\rho^{K+1}} = \frac{1 - 5/6}{1 - (5/6)^{5}} = \frac{1/6}{1 - 0.4019} = \frac{0.1667}{0.5981} \approx 0.2787$$

**Step 3: P<sub>4</sub> (lot-full probability).**

$$P_{4} = \rho^{4} \, P_{0} = (0.8333)^{4} \times 0.2787 = 0.4823 \times 0.2787 \approx 0.1344$$

**Step 4: Effective arrival rate λ<sub>e</sub>.**

$$\lambda_{e} = \lambda (1 - P_{K}) = 10 \times (1 - 0.1344) = 8.656 \text{ cars/hour}$$

**Step 5: L<sub>s</sub>.**

$$L_{s} = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} - \frac{(K+1)\rho^{K+1}}{1-\rho^{K+1}} = \frac{0.8333}{0.1667} - \frac{5 \times 0.4019}{0.5981} = 5.000 - 3.360 = 1.640 \text{ cars}$$

**Step 6: L<sub>q</sub>.**

$$L_{q} = L_{s} - (1 - P_{0}) = 1.640 - (1 - 0.2787) = 1.640 - 0.7213 = 0.919 \text{ cars}$$

**Step 7: W<sub>s</sub> and W<sub>q</sub> by Little's Law.**

$$W_{s} = \frac{L_{s}}{\lambda_{e}} = \frac{1.640}{8.656} \approx 0.1895 \text{ hours} \approx 11.37 \text{ minutes}$$

$$W_{q} = W_{s} - \frac{1}{\mu} = 0.1895 - 0.0833 = 0.1062 \text{ hours} \approx 6.37 \text{ minutes}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** In a finite-capacity model, you **must** use **λ<sub>e</sub>** (not λ) in Little's Law. Using raw λ gives the wrong W<sub>s</sub> and W<sub>q</sub> and is a guaranteed marks loser.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Feature | M/M/1 | M/M/c | M/M/∞ | M/M/1/K | M/M/c/K |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Servers | 1 | c | ∞ | 1 | c |
| Capacity | ∞ | ∞ | ∞ | K | K |
| ρ = λ/μ | <1 | <1 | any | any | any |
| $P_0$ | $1-\rho$ | Erlang-C form | $e^{-\lambda/\mu}$ | finite geom. sum | finite sum |
| $P_n$ | $(1-\rho)\rho^{n}$ | piecewise | Poisson | $(1-\rho)\rho^{n}/(1-\rho^{K+1})$ | piecewise |
| $L_s$ diverges when | ρ → 1 | ρ → 1 | never | always finite | always finite |
| Block possible? | No | No | No | Yes (P_K) | Yes (P_K) |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Queuing System

```mermaid
graph LR
    src((Calling Source)) --> arr[Arrival Process]
    arr --> q{Queue Discipline FCFS}
    q --> srv1[Server 1]
    q --> srv2[Server 2]
    q --> srvN[Server c]
    srv1 --> out((Departure))
    srv2 --> out
    srvN --> out
    capGate{Capacity K} -.-> q
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The calling source (population) generates arrivals following a stochastic process (typically Poisson). These enter the **queue discipline block** (FCFS by default), where the **capacity gate** enforces the maximum system size K. Available servers (1, 2, ..., c) process the waiting customers in parallel. Completed services exit through the **departure node**.

## 4.2 State Transition Diagram for M/M/1 Birth-Death Process

```mermaid
graph LR
    st0((State 0)) -- lambda --> st1((State 1))
    st1((State 1)) -- lambda --> st2((State 2))
    st2((State 2)) -- lambda --> st3((State 3))
    st3((State 3)) -- lambda --> stDots((State n))
    stDots((State n)) -- lambda --> stN((State n+1))
    stN((State n+1)) -- mu --> stDots2((State n))
    stDots2((State n)) -- mu --> st3r((State 3))
    st3r((State 3)) -- mu --> st2r((State 2))
    st2r((State 2)) -- mu --> st1r((State 1))
    st1r((State 1)) -- mu --> st0r((State 0))
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Each circle represents a system state (number of customers present). Rightward arrows labeled **λ** denote arrivals (births); leftward arrows labeled **μ** denote service completions (deaths). The steady-state probability of being in each state satisfies $P_{n+1} = (\lambda / \mu) P_n = \rho \cdot P_n$.

## 4.3 Comparative Flow Chart — Choosing the Right Model

```mermaid
graph TD
    start([New Queuing Problem]) --> q1{Are arrival and service times exponential?}
    q1 -- No --> stopA((Use G/G/c or simulation))
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{How many servers are there?}
    q2 -- One --> q3{Is capacity finite?}
    q2 -- c parallel --> q4{Is capacity finite?}
    q2 -- Infinite --> path5((Use M/M/infty formulas))
    q3 -- No --> path1((Use M/M/1 formulas))
    q3 -- Yes --> path2((Use M/M/1 slash K formulas))
    q4 -- No --> path3((Use M/M/c Erlang C formulas))
    q4 -- Yes --> path4((Use M/M/c slash K Erlang B formulas))
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The decision tree guides a student from problem statement to the correct classical model. The first branch checks the **Markovian** property; the second counts the servers; the third identifies whether **finite capacity** applies (look for words like "waiting room", "parking lot", "buffer size", "trunk lines").

## 4.4 Block Diagram of the Performance Measure Relationships

```mermaid
graph LR
    lam[Lambda arrival] --> ll[Little's Law L equals lambda W]
    ll --> ls[L sub s]
    ll --> ws[W sub s]
    ll --> lq[L sub q]
    ll --> wq[W sub q]
    ls -- subtract one --> lq
    ws -- subtract one over mu --> wq
    ls -- lambda times --> wqSub[Lq equals lambda Wq]
    lsSub[Ls equals lambda Ws] --> ws
    wq -- lambda times --> lqSub
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The hub of all relationships is **Little's Law** ($L = \lambda W$). The expected number in the queue is related to that in the system by $L_s = L_q + \rho$ (or $L_s = L_q + \lambda/\mu$ for M/M/c). Time measures follow the same additive pattern: $W_s = W_q + 1/\mu$.
